sources.summary: The paper describes the LMC as a low-metallicity component of the Magellanic System whose interactions shape tidal gas; UV absorption and metallicity enhancements indicate some Stream material was stripped from the LMC.

quotes.quote: The Magellanic System, including the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), Magellanic Bridge, Leading Arm (LA) and Magellanic Stream offer a nearby example ( 50 - 60 kpc ; Keller Wood 2006) of an environment with interstellar conditions that sharply contrast what we find in the Milky Way (MW). In particular, the low metallicity of the Magellanic System (e.g., 10 and 45 Solar metallicity respectively in the SMC and LMC; Rolleston et al. 2002) imply that heating and cooling mechanisms, dust-to-gas ratios, and chemical abundances may be representative of less-evolved systems at high redshift.

The LA, stretching out in front of the LMC and SMC, and the extensive, 150 ^ long Stream (Nidever et al. 2010) trailing behind them are understood as features of tidal (e.g. Murai Fujimoto 1980, Yozin Bekki 2014) and/or ram-pressure stripping (e.g., Moore Davis 1994, Mastropietro et al. 2005) interactions between the SMC, LMC and MW. Recent UV absorption measurements support the scenario that the Stream originated from the SMC, and observed metallicity enhancements indicate some material has been stripped from LMC (Fox et al. 2013, 2014, Richter et al. 2013).

There is a wealth of evidence for star formation within Magellanic structures outside the LMC and SMC, including diffuse H emission (e.g., Meaburn 1986), and massive, young stars in the LA (e.g., Casetti-Dinescu et al. 2014) and Bridge (e.g., Demers Battinelli 1998), while the Stream remains starless.

sources.summary: The review presents the LMC as a low-metallicity irregular dwarf whose extinction curve lies between Milky Way and SMC behavior, with regional variations and mid-IR measurements from Spitzer/SAGE.

quotes.quote: Like the SMC, the LMC is also a low-metalicity irregular dwarf galaxy and a satellite of the MW. Since the metallicity of the LMC (which is only 1/4 of that of the MW; Russell Dopita 1992) is similar to that of galaxies at red shifts z 1 (see Dobashi et al. \ 2008), it offers opportunities to study the dust properties in distant low-metallicity extragalactic environments by studying the extinction properties of the LMC. As illustrated in Figure 7, the LMC extinction curve is intermediate between that of the MW and that of the SMC: compared to the Galactic extinction curve, the LMC extinction curve is characterized by a weaker 2175 hump and a stronger far-UV rise (Nandy 1981, Koornneef Code 1981, Gordon et al. \ 2003). Strong regional variations in extinction properties have also been found in the LMC (Clayton Martin 1985; Fitzpatrick 1985, 1986; Misselt et al. \ 1999; Gordon et al. \ 2003): the sightlines toward the stars inside or near the supergiant shell, LMC 2, which lies on the southeast side of the 30 Doradus star-forming region, have a weak 2175 hump (Misselt et al. \ 1999), while the extinction curves for the sightlines toward the stars which are > 500 pc away from the 30 Doradus region are closer to the Galactic extinction curve. Based on the photometric data from the Spitzer /SAGE survey and with red giants as the extinction tracer, Gao et al. \ (2013) derived the mid-IR extinction for a number of regions of the LMC in the four IRAC bands.

sources.summary: The paper compares integrated-light and resolved-star methods for a bar field in the LMC, finding STECKMAP recovers consistent star-formation and age-metallicity histories.

quotes.quote: We have obtained a high signal--to--noise (S/N 36.3 per ) integrated spectrum of a field in the bar of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) using EFOSC2 at the 3.6 meter telescope at La Silla Observatory. For this same field, resolved stellar data reaching the oMSTO are available. We have compared the star formation rate (SFR) as a function of time and the age-metallicity relation (AMR) obtained from the integrated spectrum using STECKMAP , and the CMD using the IAC-star/MinnIAC/IAC-pop set of routines. For the sake of completeness we also use and discuss other synthesis codes ( STARLIGHT and ULySS ) to derive the SFR and AMR from the integrated LMC spectrum. We find very good agreement (average differences 4.1 ) between the SFR(t) and the AMR obtained using STECKMAP on the integrated light spectrum, and the CMD analysis.

sources.summary: The review describes the LMC as a nearby dwarf satellite where H.E.S.S. resolved individual VHE gamma-ray sources and expects future TeV discoveries with CTA.

quotes.quote: The Large Magellanic Cloud As a final point on Galactic sources and moving to the extragalactic sky, the Large Magellanic Cloud, a dwarf satellite galaxy of our Milky Way, is extremely instructive and encouraging for the future.

detected three extremely energetic objects of different type within the LMC : the PWN N 157B, a counterpart to the Crab nebula but with an electron acceleration efficiency is five times lower, the SNR N 132D, one of the oldest VHE gamma-ray emitting SNRs, the superbubble 30 Dor C, thus putting into light a new class of VHE emitters. It is the first time in a galaxy outside the Milky Way, that individual sources of very high energy gamma-rays can be resolved. Interestingly, the unique object SN 1987A is, surprisingly, not detected, which constrains the theoretical framework of particle acceleration in very young supernova remnants. These results open a new window at TeV energies and we can expect further discoveries with more sensitive surveys of the LMC in gamma-rays, for instance with the Cherenkov Telescope Array.

sources.summary: The paper uses LMCNISS near-infrared Cepheid data and Galactic calibrations to derive an independent LMC distance modulus of 18.47 +/- 0.07 mag, consistent with eclipsing-binary results.

quotes.quote: Determining a robust distance to the LMC is an important step in the cosmic distance scale. Recently, piet13 used a sample of 8 eclipsing binaries to obtain a 2.2 accurate distance to the LMC of D = 49.97 1.11 kpc (equivalent to _ LMC = 18.493 0.048 mag). One of the motivations for our work is to provide an independent determination of the LMC distance modulus by applying a Galactic calibration to data from the Large Magellanic Cloud NIR Synoptic Survey (LMCNISS) hereafter Paper I macri15 .

We use the new LMCNISS data to provide an independent estimate of the distance to the LMC. Using Galactic calibrations, we determine _ LMC = 18.47 , with a total statistical uncertainty of 0.07 mag, which is in excellent agreement with the value from piet13 based on late-type eclipsing binaries.

sources.summary: The paper compares the LMC dust budget with supernova yields, estimating AGB production, dust lifetimes, interstellar dust mass, and a destruction rate that implies gas-phase dust accretion.

quotes.quote: Because of source confusion in the Galactic plane, surveys have targeted the Magellanic Clouds to make a global census of dusty AGB stars, inferring dust production rates of 2 10^ 5 M_ /yr for the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC, 2012ApJ...753...71R ) and of 8 10^ 7 M_ /yr for the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC, 2012ApJ...748...40B ), with variations among the results of different surveys ( 2013MNRAS.429.2527M ), and in good agreement with theoretical predictions ( 2012MNRAS.420.1442V,2012MNRAS.424.2345V,2014MNRAS.439..977V ). Hence, even in the LMC, the current dust production rate by AGB stars appears to be more than one order of magnitude smaller than that estimated from our sample of four SNe.

Rescaling these values by the ratio between the ISM mass in the Milky Way and in the LMC ( M_ ISM, MW / M_ ISM, LMC 100 ), the dust lifetime in the LMC can be estimated as 10^6 - 10^7 yr, in agreement with the recent observational study by , which leads to _ des, LMC 2 10^7 yr. Then, assuming a total interstellar dust mass in the LMC of M_ dust = 7.3 10^5 M_ ( 2014ApJ...797...85G ), the resulting dust destruction rate is dM_ des, LMC / dt 3 10^ 2 7 10^ 1 M_ yr ^ 1 . These values are larger than the inferred dust production rates for SNe and AGB stars by a factor of > 100 , therefore requiring dust accretion in the gas phase of the LMC.

sources.summary: The chapter treats the LMC as the most massive Milky Way satellite, a dwarf-irregular member of the LMC-SMC pair whose binary interaction explains features including the Magellanic Stream.

quotes.quote: The most massive Milky Way satellite, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) , is itself part of a bound pair of dwarf irregular galaxies (with the Small Magellanic Cloud, or SMC Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) ).

In fact, many of the unique features of the LMC-SMC Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) pair Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) can be explained by their binary interaction e.g., besla2012 , including the spectacular 200 ^ Magellanic Stream Magellanic Stream in HI nidever2010 . Binary pairs are somewhat rare, as robotham2012 find only two MW+LMC+SMC analog systems among the Galaxy Mass Assembly (GAMA) galaxies. While only 3.4 percent of GAMA galaxies are MW+LMC+SMC analogs, 12 of SDSS Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) galaxies have LMC-like companions i.e., luminous satellite within 75 kpc; tollerud2011 , which suggests that about one in four LMC-like satellites have a smaller SMC-like companion. The relatively low fraction of LMC-SMC binary satellite systems supports a ``transient'' nature, as detailed numerical simulations of the LMC-SMC suggest they may not remain a bound pair for long besla2012 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses the LMC as the Milky Way's brightest satellite, testing negligible and massive-LMC scenarios and noting that HST proper motions favor a substantial total LMC mass.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, in our Galaxy _ acc may be dominated by the (total) mass of the brightest satellite galaxy, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), which to date remains strongly debated in the literature. Let us inspect two leading scenarios, one where the LMC contribution to the Galaxy mass is negligible, and a second one where the total LMC mass is introduced as a free parameter in the Hubble flow model. Using the SCM to fit the dynamics of nearby ( 0.8 R/ 3 ) galaxies under the assumption M_ LMC 0 yields M_ MW,lhf =0.8^ +0.4 _ 0.3 10^ 12 M_ (P14).

In contrast, P16 find that the LMC may be more massive than previously thought (see also Besla et al. 2012 and Belokurov Koposov 2016). Implementing the proper motions of the LMC measured recently from HST data in Equation ( eq:angmom2 ) and fitting M_ LMC and M_ MW, lfh simultaneously to the same data set as in P14 returns a similar effective Milky Way mass, M_ MW,lhf =1.04^ +0.42 _ 0.38 10^ 12 M_ , while strongly favouring a massive LMC, M_ LMC =0.25^ +0.09 _ 0.08 10^ 12 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper studies carbon and oxygen abundance gradients across the LMC, finding nearly flat O/H, C/H, and C/O slopes and comparing its evolution to outer small-spiral regions.

quotes.quote: We studied the spatial distribution of O and C abundances as a function of the fractional galactocentric distances ( R/R_ 25 ) in the LMC and SMC. Table tab:fits shows the radial abundance gradients for both galaxies. We find that the slope of the O/H gradient is almost the same using CELs or RLs and can be considered essentially flat in both galaxies. Moreover, the slope of C/H and C/O gradients in both galaxies can also be considered practically zero, in contrast with the results that are found in spiral galaxies (e.g. ).

The position of the H ii \ regions in the figure suggests that the chemical evolution of the SMC behaves as a typical star-forming dwarf galaxy, while the LMC seems to be similar to the external zones of small spiral galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper discusses the LMC as interacting with the SMC, reproducing its off-center bar and HI spirals in simulations, and possibly receiving transferred SMC stars into diffuse halo components.

quotes.quote: Thus, the gravitational interaction between the Small and the Large Magellanic Clouds (SMC and LMC, respectively) may be playing a larger role than the MW in triggering star formation.

The MCs simulations of 2007PASA...24...21B over the last 800 Myr are able to reproduce the off-center bar and the HI spirals in the LMC. They also predict that a substantial number of SMC stars could be transferred to the LMC to form diffuse halo components around that galaxy.

2012MNRAS.421.2109B present two models for the Magellanic System, designed to explain the MS as the action of LMC tides on the SMC. In their models the LMC is a one-armed spiral and features as well a warped, off-centre stellar bar as a result of the gravitational interaction.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as part of the interacting Magellanic pair and argues that its coherent magnetic field may have been pulled into the Magellanic Bridge.

quotes.quote: The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) are a highly-studied galaxy pair. Due to their close proximity to the Milky Way (MW), the Magellanic Clouds allow astronomers to study galaxy interactions and evolution in unprecedented detail. The on-going interaction between the galaxy pair, and possibly the MW, have led to the creation of the Magellanic Bridge (MB), the Magellanic Stream, and the Leading Arm (see Besla2010 and D'OnghiaFox2016 for a complete review).
Studies of Faraday rotation of background polarized radio sources towards the LMC have determined that the galaxy has a coherent magnetic field of strength 1 G Gaensler2005 .
More recently, Mao2008 and LoboGomes2015 note the potential alignment of the SMC magnetic field with the MB, and Mao2012 argue the same for the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Fermi LAT data for the LMC and reports a spectral break around 500 MeV in its disk gamma-ray emission, consistent with pion-decay models.

quotes.quote: We here report the evidence of a spectral break around 500 MeV in the disk emission of Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), which is found in the analysis of the gamma-ray data extending down to 60 MeV observed by Fermi Large Area Telescope. The break is well consistent with the pion-decay model for the gamma-ray emission, although leptonic models, such as the electron bremsstrahlung emission, cannot be ruled out completely.

The LMC is the brightest external galaxies in gamma-ray emission, as it is very close to us (only 50 kpc). The LMC is near enough that individual star-forming regions can be resolved and thus their contribution can be removed so that one can obtain a relatively pure diffuse disk component. The high Galactic latitude of the LMC also leads to a low level of contamination due to the Galactic diffuse gamma-ray emission.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as the Milky Way's most massive satellite and a perturbing body whose recent capture and orbital plane may affect Draco and Sculptor orbital integrations.

quotes.quote: We also study the influence of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) on the orbits of Draco and Sculptor. Overall, the inclusion of the LMC increases the scatter in the orbital results.

This picture is further complicated by perturbations from the MW's most massive satellite galaxy, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). It is unclear whether the gravitational pull of the LMC might complicate the orbits of the classical satellites, causing their membership to the plane of satellites to be unstable. The recent capture of the LMC by the MW bes07,kal13 may limit its dynamical influence on the DoS members, but this is impossible to properly assess without accurate PM measurements for the classical dwarfs.

sources.summary: The LMC is presented as the larger Magellanic dwarf interacting with the SMC; the paper finds Bridge stars mainly SMC-like, with at most one object kinematically compatible with LMC membership.

quotes.quote: The Magellanic Bridge (MB) is a gaseous stream that links the Large (LMC) and Small (SMC) Magellanic Clouds. Current simulations suggest that the MB forms from a recent interaction between the Clouds. In this scenario, the MB should also have an associated stellar bridge formed by stars tidally stripped from the SMC by the LMC.

We have shown that stars older than 1 Gyr in the MB region, with radial velocities larger than 100 km s ^ 1 , are unequivocally linked with the SMC. Except for one star with a velocity of 270 km s ^ 1 , we do not find objects with kinematics compatible with that of the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper applies the same red-clump extinction analysis to the LMC 30 Doradus region, concluding that line-of-sight depth can explain the apparent gray extinction offset.

quotes.quote: Similar extinction curve offsets in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) have been interpreted as the effect of large dust grains. We demonstrate that the line-of-sight depth of the SMC (and LMC) introduces an apparent ``gray'' contribution to the extinction curve inferred from the morphology of the red clump. We show that no gray dust component is needed to explain extinction curve measurements when a full-width half-max depth of 10 2 kpc in the stellar distribution of the SMC (5 1 kpc for the LMC) is considered, which agrees with recent studies of Magellanic Cloud stellar structure.

Motivated by recent extinction curve shape results for 30 Dor in the Large Magellanic Cloud by DeMarchi:2014fe and DeMarchi:2016 who use the same red clump method and also report an offset, we perform the same analysis on 30 Dor in the LMC. Our conclusions for the optical and near-infrared portions of the extinction curve shape in both the SMC and the LMC is consistent with previous work such as the analysis of Gordon:2003 using spectroscopic measurements.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Magellanic Clouds as nearby microlensing source fields whose self-lensing, LMC-SMC streams, and 3-D structure complicate dark-matter constraints.

quotes.quote: Trigged by Paczyński's seminal paper, several experiments such as MACHO [ 5 ] , Expérience pour la Recherche d'Objets Sombres (EROS [ 6 ] ), Optical Gravitational Lensing Experiment (OGLE [ 7 ] ), Microlensing Observations in Astrophysics (MOA, [ 8 ] ) were carried out, aiming at the closest dense stellar fields — Magellanic Clouds. After the first detections, the MACHO team continuously observed the Magellanic Clouds until the devastating bush fire destroyed the 50-inch Great Melbourne Telescope in January 2003.

Using the data gathered by OGLE in 1998-2000 (OGLE-II) and 2002-2009 (OGLE-III), Wyrzykowski et al. [ 15-17 ] also concluded that microlensing events towards both Magellanic Clouds can be well explained by self-lensing (both the lens and source are stars in the Magellanic Clouds) without invoking compact dark matter. Even more so, Besla et al. [ 18 ] presented studies of tidal streams between LMC and SMC, and showed that microlensing signals can be reproduced by stars in the stream.
In addition, because of the proximity of Magellanic Clouds, their 3-D structures are non-negligible and the self-lensing rate is unknown.

sources.summary: The paper notes the LMC is the brightest external gamma-ray galaxy, with diffuse high-energy disk emission observed by Fermi LAT and individual luminous point sources now detectable.

quotes.quote: The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is the brightest external galaxy in the gamma-ray band, as it is very close to us (only about 49.97 kpc). High-energy (HE, > 100 MeV) diffusive gamma rays in the LMC, which are thought to originate from the large-scale disk, are mainly produced by hadronic process  2015ApJ...808...44F,2016A&A...586A..71A,2017ApJ...843...42T . This disk component contributes a large amount of gamma rays of LMC observed by Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT, 20 MeV to above 300 GeV). However, the details on the distribution and origin of the CRs cannot be revealed without detections of the individual sources in the LMC. Recently, a few luminous point sources has been detected in the LMC, thanks to high-energy and very-high-energy (VHE, > 100 GeV) telescopes.

sources.summary: The paper models clustered-supernova outflows in an LMC-like dwarf disk, arguing ram pressure can turn fountains into expelled gas contributing to the LMC filament in the Trailing Stream.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we present controlled, 3D simulations of ram pressure stripping combined with thermally driven, local outflows from clustered supernovae in an isolated disk galaxy modeled on the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), a dwarf satellite of the Milky Way on its first infall. Observational evidence of local outflows emanating from supergiant shells in the LMC and a trailing filament of HI gas originating from these regions - with no obvious Leading Arm counterpart - may represent a perfect example of this process. Our simulations present a proof-of-concept that ram pressure can convert fountain flows into expelled gas. We find that fountains launched near the peak star formation time of the LMC can comprise part of the LMC filament in the Trailing Stream, but with lower column densities than observed. Larger, more numerous outflows from the LMC may be possible and may contribute more mass, but higher inertia gas will lengthen the timescale for this gas to be swept away by ram pressure.

sources.summary: The paper contrasts the LMC with Sgr, emphasizing the LMC’s likely first approach, retained gas, recent tidal stripping, and role in the Magellanic Stream.

quotes.quote: New mass determinations now render the precursors of Sgr muc17a,gib17a comparable to the LMC jet16a , with combined dark-matter (DM) and baryonic masses of up to 10^ 11 . Why have the Magellanic Clouds retained much of their gas while Sgr has apparently lost it all? A key difference between Sgr and the LMC is their orbital history. The LMC is likely on its first approach to the Galaxy shu92a,byr94a,bes07a , currently at an estimated distance from the Galactic centre of d 50 kpc pie13b . Models of the evolution of Sgr from infall to its present location at d 16 - 20 kpc or D 25 - 29 kpc from the Sun; q.v. kun09a agree that Sgr has undergone already several pericentric passages e.g. fel06a,law10a,nie12a .

The LMC began to experience significant tidal gas stripping assisted by ram pressure due to the tenuous corona of the Galaxy 10^ 4 at 50 kpc; e.g. tep15a only recently 1.5 Gyr; gug14a , as attested by the prominent Magellanic Stream that extends from the MCs over nearly 200 \ across the sky mat74a,nid10a .

sources.summary: The paper describes the LMC as an interacting dwarf satellite whose tidal interaction with the SMC formed Magellanic structures, and notes its clockwise disc rotation from proper-motion studies.

quotes.quote: The Large and the Small Magellanic Cloud (LMC and SMC) are the most prominent dwarf galaxy satellites of the Milky Way and are in the early phases of a minor merger event. Tidal interactions between the two Clouds led to the formation of the gaseous Magellanic Stream see e.g., Nidever08, D'Onghia16 and the Magellanic Bridge e.g. Irwin85 which is composed of stars and gas, and connects the two dwarf galaxies. Thanks to their close vicinity, the Clouds provide a unique opportunity for studying in detail the stellar kinematics within an interacting pair of galaxies.

The internal proper motion field of the LMC, which was measured by vanderMarel14 for the first time, reveals a clockwise rotation of the LMC disc in the plane of the sky, consistent with previous line-of-sight velocity measurements. This result was confirmed by vanderMarel16 using the proper motions from the Tycho-Gaia Astrometric Solution (TGAS) Catalog Michalik15,Lindegren16 .

sources.summary: The paper evaluates the LMC as a possible origin for LAMOST-HVS4, finding trajectory distances and flight times that make an origin in its core unlikely.

quotes.quote: For LMC and Sgr dSph, we further calculate the nearest distances and flight times of 50,000 orbits. If LAMOST-HVS4 is an MS star, the nearest distances to the LMC and Sgr dSph are 37.9 0.6 and 24.1 0.7 kpc, and the flight times are 90 3 and 48 2 Myr. The flight time of 90 3 Myr is larger than its lifetime, thus it is impossible for it to arrive at the LMC within its lifetime. If it is a BHB star, the nearest distances to LMC and Sgr dSph are 35.5 0.8 and 24.4 0.8 kpc, and the flight times are 73 6 and 17 7 Myr. The tidal radii of LMC and Sgr dSph are 22.3 5.2 and 3 kpc 2014ApJ...781..121V, 2001MNRAS.323..529H , respectively, thus LAMOST-HVS4 is unlikely originated from the core regions of LMC and Sgr dSph, but the dwarf galaxy origin cannot be exclude completely.

sources.summary: The paper describes the LMC as a luminous dwarf satellite bound to and recently interacting with the SMC, in a system now merging with the Milky Way; Bridge kinematics support SMC stripping by the LMC.

quotes.quote: The Large and Small Magellanic Cloud (LMC and SMC) are the most luminous dwarf galaxy satellites of the Milky Way (MW). They are most likely gravitationally bound to each other, and their last interaction occurred about 200 Myr ago. Also, they are in an early phase of minor merging with the MW and will impact the Galactic structure in the future because of their relatively large mass. However, there are still major uncertainties regarding their origin and their interactions with one another and with the Milky Way.
Our analysis supports a motion of star towards the LMC, which was found to be in good agreement with a dynamical simulation, of the SMC being stripped by the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper uses the LMC as a nearby face-on laboratory, describing its molecular-cloud surveys, the N159 GMC as an active star-forming site, and kpc-scale H I flows driven by LMC-SMC tidal interaction.

quotes.quote: In this regard, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is close to us at a distance of 50 kpc Schaefer08,de14 , and its face-on view Balbinot15 allows us to isolate associations among the molecular clouds, massive YSOs, and H ii regions without the abovementioned contamination problem. Extensive molecular gas surveys toward the LMC identified 300 giant molecular clouds (GMCs) Fukui99,Fukui08,Kawamura09,Fukui10 and found that the N159 GMC is a crucial object as the most active ongoing star formation site among the samples.

The largest giant molecular complex in the LMC, called `` the molecular ridge, " which is shown in green contours in Figures fig:preHICO (a) and (b), is mainly located at the intermediate velocity range between the L- and D-components Fukui17 . These authors proposed that the tidal interaction between the LMC and the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) drove a kpc H i flow, which originated in the past close encounter between the two galaxies, which was modeled in the numerical simulations by following the original suggestion by . After settling outside the LMC disk for 200 Myr, the gas could be now inducing the formation of the super star cluster R136 in 30 Dor as well as the molecular ridge containing the N159 region Fukui17 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies the LMC as the SMC's companion and part of the interacting Magellanic system, whose proximity shapes SMC outflow dynamics, tidal radius estimates, and Magellanic Stream formation.

quotes.quote: We suggest that most of the observed outflow will be stripped from the SMC through its interaction with its companion, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), and the Milky Way, feeding the Magellanic Stream of hydrogen encircling the Milky Way.

The gaseous structure of the main body of the SMC is described by two dominant regions as marked on Figure fig:peak_intensity : the so-called bar, extending from the north to the south-west, and containing the majority of the dense gas and star formation and the wing, which extends in the direction of the LMC.

In addition, the presence of the Milky Way and LMC in close proximity to the SMC alter the dynamics of the system. The Magellanic Stream and Leading Arm are evidence of tidal and/or ram pressure interactions between the LMC/SMC system and the Milky Way. The tidal radius of the SMC is difficult to determine given the complex interaction with the LMC, uncertainties in the relative masses of the SMC, LMC and Milky Way, and recent revisions in the proper motions of the Magellanic Clouds.

sources.summary: The paper reports LPV-based star-formation histories for the Magellanic Clouds, finding earlier bulk star formation in the LMC and a shared secondary peak.

quotes.quote: This new technique was successfully used in M 33 (Javadi \ 2011a,b, 2017), the Magellanic Clouds (Rezaeikh \ 2014), NGC 147 NGC 185 (Golshan \ 2017) and IC 1613 (Hashemi \ 2018). The main results that we found in these galaxies are summarized as below: The disc of M 33 was built > 6 Gyr ago, when most stars in M 33 73 were formed. The second enhanced epoch of star formation in M 33 occurred 250 Myr ago and contributed 6 to M 33's historic star formation. Radial star formation history profiles suggest that the inner disc of M 33 was formed in an inside--out formation scenario. We found a significant difference in the ancient SFH of the LMC and the SMC. For the SMC the bulk of the stars formed a few Gyr later than the LMC. A secondary peak of SFH at 700 Myr ago in the LMC and the SMC is possibly due to the tidal interaction between the Magellanic Clouds and their approach to the Milky Way.

sources.summary: The paper tests whether the newly discovered halo star cluster originated with the Large Magellanic Cloud, comparing its position and proper motion with LMC satellite-infall simulations and judging an LMC origin unlikely.

quotes.quote: Combining the available positional and velocity information with simulations of the accreted satellite population of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), we find that it is unlikely that originated with the LMC.

resides in a region of the sky where the gravitational influence of the LMC is non-negligible. While is currently located 40.5 ( 53 5 in projection) from the LMC, it is natural to consider whether may have originated with the LMC before being tidally captured by the Milky Way during accretion of the LMC. We test this hypothesis by comparing with numerical simulations from Jethwa:2016 , which model the evolution of the LMC satellite population during infall. These simulations sample plausible ranges of LMC orbital histories and initial total masses for the Milky Way ( M_ MW ) and LMC ( M_ LMC ).

sources.summary: The paper reconstructs the LMC star-formation history from LPV stars, finding most stars formed about 10 Gyr ago and a later peak linked to Magellanic interaction.

quotes.quote: For the nearby, relatively massive and interacting gas-rich dwarf galaxies, the Magellanic Clouds, we found that the bulk of the stars formed 10 Gyr ago for the LMC, while the strongest episode of star formation in the SMC occurred a few Gyr later. A peak in star formation around 0.7 Gyr ago in both Clouds is likely linked to their recent interaction.

Results for the Magellanic Clouds were obtained based on the variable star catalogues of and with 43,551 and 19,384 LPV stars, respectively. As is shown in Fig. fig2 , the bulk of the stars formed 10 Gyr ago for the LMC, at a rate of 1.598 0.054 M _ yr ^ 1 , while for the SMC, two formation epochs at 6 Gyr, with SFR 0.282 0.017 M _ yr ^ 1 and 0.7 Gyr ago, with SFR 0.310 0.019 M _ yr ^ 1 , are observed. We derived the total stellar mass produced, 2.2 10^ 10 M _ for the LMC and 4.0 10^8 M _ for the SMC ( ).

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a live multi-component dwarf galaxy in a binary pair with the SMC, assigning it gas, stellar, and dark-matter components and initial metallicity.

quotes.quote: This model is constructed essentially in two steps: i) The LMC and SMC are evolved for 6 Gyr as a binary pair in isolation starting from some prescribed initial conditions; ii) The evolved binary system is placed into a Galaxy-like potential at one Galactic virial radius ( 220 kpc), and evolved further for roughly 1 Gyr (see Appendix sec:ver for further details). In contrast, the MCs are modelled as fully live, multi-component galaxies, each consisting of a stellar disc and a gas disc embedded in a DM host subhalo. In this model, the MCs have -- by design -- large initial gas fractions, implying that higher gas masses are deposited onto the structures that later form the Stream and the Leading Arm, compared to models with lower initial gas fractions.

The average gas metallicity of the MCs today (as given by the abundance of elements) is Z 0.3 \ (SMC) and Z 0.4 \ LMC; rus92a . Therefore, we set the initial gas metallicity of the SMC (LMC) to Z = 0.1 (0.2 ).

sources.summary: The white paper highlights the Magellanic Clouds as the nearest galaxies for spatially resolved extinction measurements, where limited UV curves already show broader dust-property variation than the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: The Magellanic Clouds provide the nearest galaxies in which we can easily measure dust extinction at different positions through a galaxy. Due to the relative faintness of stars in these galaxies, the number of UV curves measured in both galaxies is much smaller than in the MW with 20 for the LMC Misselt99 and approximately the same for the SMC Gordon et al. 2019b, in prep Gordon98, MaizAppellaniz12 The majority of these UV curves deviate strongly from those seen in the MW. The most extreme variations are found in the SMC Bar where the curves have no 2175 \ bump and a very steep UV slope, yet in this same galaxy there are sightlines with strong 2175 \ bumps. Fig. fig_all_curves shows the average LMC and SMC curves with the average curve for the MW.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a luminous dwarf whose halo should host several satellites, using Gaia motions to test which Milky Way dwarf spheroidals may share its orbital history.

quotes.quote: For galaxies as luminous as the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), and for halos as massive as expected for the LMC (determined by its rotation speed), the simulations predict about 3 satellites with stellar masses exceeding M_*>10^5 M_ . If the LMC is on its first pericentric passage, then these satellites should be near the LMC and should have orbital angular momenta roughly coincident with that of the LMC. We use 3D positions and velocities from the 2nd data release of the Gaia mission to revisit which of the ``classical'' MW dwarf spheroidals could plausibly be LMC satellites. The new proper motions of the Fornax and Carina dwarf spheroidals place them on orbits closely aligned with the orbital plane of the Magellanic Clouds, hinting at a potential Magellanic association. Together with the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), this result raises to 3 the number of LMC satellites with M_*>10^5 M_ , as expected from simulations.

sources.summary: The paper discusses the LMC as part of the Magellanic system whose interactions and gas chemistry constrain the origin of the Leading Arm and Magellanic Stream.

quotes.quote: However, the exact timing, mass, and origin (e.g., LMC or SMC) for the gas required to form the LA, MS, and the Magellanic Bridge remain unknown Pardy2018 . Due to the differences in the star formation and baryon cycle histories of the LMC and SMC, the chemical abundances of the gas provide critical clues to understanding the origin and evolution of the Magellanic system.

The current peak stellar metallicities of the LMC and SMC are [ Fe/H ] = 0.6 dex and [ Fe/H ] = 1.0 dex, and even higher in the innermost regions, which have ongoing star formation, reaching [ Fe/H ] = 0.2 and [ Fe/H ] = 0.7, respectively Nidever2019b . The current mean metallicity of the gas in the LMC and SMC is also high --- [ Fe/H ] = 0.2 and [ Fe/H ] = 0.6, respectively Russell1992 -- consistent with the most metal-rich stars.
One of the recent theoretical studies Pardy2018 argued that both the LMC and SMC contributed to create the LA and MS gas features.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes LMC dust and infrared power spectra, finding mostly single power-law behavior, a 24 micron break caused by 30 Doradus, and local index variations without robust breaks.

quotes.quote: We analyze the 1D spatial power spectra of dust surface density and mid to far-infrared emission at 24 - 500 m in the LMC, SMC, M31, and M33. By forward-modelling the point-spread-function (PSF) on the power spectrum, we find that nearly all power spectra have a single power-law and point source component. A broken power-law model is only favoured for the LMC 24 m MIPS power spectrum and is due to intense dust heating in 30 Doradus. We also test for local power spectrum variations by splitting the LMC and SMC maps into 820 pc boxes. We find significant variations in the power-law index with no strong evidence for breaks.

sources.summary: The paper treats the Large Magellanic Cloud as a massive nearby Milky Way satellite whose gravitational pull and former satellites affect the Milky Way mass estimate.

quotes.quote: An additional complication is given by the presence of a close-by and relatively massive neighbour, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), which exerts a strong gravitational pull Gomez_15, Garavito_19 . In Section sec:lmc we present our assumptions regarding the reflex motion of the MW due to the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and explore the effect of galaxies in the sample that are possible former satellites of the LMC.

In principle, a few objects could have been satellites of the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), but since the SMC was itself very likely a satellite of the LMC, we do not distinguish between the two cases.

The LMC is, however, an unusually large satellite (satellites as massive as the LMC are rare in observations and simulations, e.g. Liu_11 , Boylan-Kolchin_10 ). We therefore exclude its satellites in this test, but we do include the LMC itself, since one tracer should be used for a group.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a low-metallicity Magellanic environment for massive YSO variability, using VMC/VISTA monitoring and noting its higher gas-to-dust ratio may reduce extinction-driven amplitudes.

quotes.quote: Here we present the first variability study of massive YSOs in a 1.5 deg^2 region of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). The aim is to investigate whether the different environmental conditions in the metal-poor LMC ( 0.4-0.5 Z_ ) have an impact on the variability characteristics. Multi-epoch near-infrared (NIR) photometry was obtained from the VISTA Survey of the Magellanic Clouds (VMC) and our own monitoring campaign using the VISTA telescope.

For the LMC this ratio is 3 times larger than in the Galaxy Welty2012 , hence column density changes caused by inhomogeneities along the line-of-sight would lead to smaller changes in extinction. Consequently, for YSOs where extinction plays any role in causing photometric variability, a lower metallicity might actually have the effect of reducing the amplitudes.

sources.summary: The paper frames the LMC as a massive first-infall satellite whose high velocity, large mass, and dark-matter wake perturb Milky Way halo dynamics.

quotes.quote: One major source of disequilibrium in the MW is its most massive satellite, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). The classical picture of the LMC is as a relatively low mass ( 10^ 10 M_ ) satellite orbiting the MW on a T 2 Gyr orbit (e.g., Avner1967 , Hunter1969 , Murai1980 , Lin1982 , Lin1995 , Bekki2005 , Mastropietro2005 , Connors2006 ). However, proper motion measurements of the LMC using the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) revealed that the total velocity of the LMC is much higher than previously measured ( v 320 ; Kallivayalil2006 , Kallivayalil2013 ). This high velocity, near the escape speed of the MW, indicates that the LMC is likely on its first infall, based on backward orbital integrations ( Besla2007 , Kallivayalil2013 ) and statistical predictions from cosmological simulations ( Boylan-Kolchin2011 , Busha2011 , Gonzalez2013 , Patel2017 ). In addition, there is mounting evidence that the LMC is more massive than previously thought ( 10^ 11 M_ ), including arguments based on models of the Magellanic system (e.g., Besla2010 , Besla2012 , Pardy2018 ); abundance matching (e.g., Behroozi2010 , Guo2010 , Moster2010 , Moster2013 ); the measured rotation curve of the LMC vanderMarel2014 ; the presence of satellites around the LMC, including the Small Magellanic Cloud (e.g., Kallivayalil2018 , Erkal2019a , Pardy2019 , Patel2020 ); the timing argument ( Penarrubia2016 ); and perturbations in the Orphan Stream ( Koposov2019 , Erkal2019b ).

sources.summary: The paper models the LMC as a massive satellite whose inclusion changes globular-cluster orbits; together with the SMC, it is identified as a dominant orbital perturber.

quotes.quote: Gomez_2015 studied the importance of considering the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) in simulations of the tidal tails of the Sagittarius dwarf galaxy (Sgr) around the Milky Way. They found that when the LMC was included, tidal debris from Sgr was found to be misaligned with the present-day orbital plane of Sgr. More generally, Patel20 finds that interactions with the Milky Way and the Magellanic Clouds can affect both the orbital history and evolution of other satellites. Even more subtle changes to the external tidal field, like how the Milky Way's halo itself responds to satellite galaxies GaravitoCamargo19 , can lead to the perturbation of satellite orbits. With respect to GCs, Erkal2018 and Erkal2019 demonstrated that the Large Magellanic Cloud is capable of perturbing the orbits of the Tucana and Orphan streams, respectively.
However we do find evidence that the SMC and LMC are the dominant source of orbital perturbations.

sources.summary: The paper finds LMC planetary nebulae often density bounded, with high line-ratio values, resolved oxygen doublets, and H-line ratios consistent with expectations.

quotes.quote: We find an anticorrelation between the electron densities and the ionized masses in M 31, M 33, and NGC 300 which suggests that most of the PNe observed in these galaxies are probably ionization bounded. This trend is absent in LMC and SMC suggesting that many of their PNe are density bounded. The / values found in many PNe in LMC and some in M 33 and SMC are higher than in the other galaxies. Photoionization models predict that these high values can only be reached in density bounded PNe.

We have performed this correction in the spectra of the PNe of the following galaxies: M 31 kwitter12, balick13, corradi15 , M 33 magrini09, bresolin10 , NGC 300 stasinska13 , NGC 3109 pena07a , LMC leisy06 , SMC leisy06, shaw10 , NGC 4449 annibali17 and NGC 6822 richer07, hdezmtnez09 ; all these spectra have a spectral resolution greater than 2--3 \ and a detected line at 4711 . A more detailed discussion on this correction has been done in Section nature . Lines of [ O ii ] 3726+29 are summed in the tables even if observations allow one to separate the lines. The only PNe where this doublet is resolved are: one object in NGC 3109 observed by pena07a and the sample studied by floresduran17 , the PNe in the LMC and SMC reported by tsamis03 , and the SMC sample observed by shaw10 .

The H / line ratio reported in the figure is in agreement with the theoretically expected value for some of the galaxies: M 31, NGC 300, NGC 3109, LMC, SMC, either because the reddening was obtained using these lines precisely or because the observers paid special attention to it. But it is very far from it in NGC 205, M 32, M 81, and many objects in M 33. The first five galaxies have a value of the weighted standard deviation of H / divided by its theoretical value 0.01 while this value is 0.03 for NGC 205, M 32, M 81, and M 33.

sources.summary: The paper compares M31 stars to Magellanic Cloud abundances, noting the LMC is more metal rich, has an ancient metal-poor tail, and models imply low early star-formation efficiency.

quotes.quote: The LMC is more metal-rich with 0.68 , reaching metallicities as high as 0.2, compared to the SMC ( 0.97 ), which has a maximum metallicity of 0.5 .

Chemical evolution models for the LMC (and by extension, the SMC, owing to their similar chemical abundance patterns) predict the dominance of Type Ia supernovae over core-collapse supernovae during this quiescent epoch BekkiTsujimoto2012,Nidever2019 , resulting in the observed decline in \ with \ in the metal-poor tails of the LMC's abundance distribution . These tails are comparably ancient to the dSph-like stars: the predicted age-metallicity relation from Nidever2019 has the LMC reaching 1.5 at 6 Gyr into its evolution Nidever2019 , suggesting that the majority of its metal-poor tail was in place by 7 Gyr ago. The models of BekkiTsujimoto2012 reach 1.5 by 9-11 Gyr ago, but were not well-constrained at low metallicity by the available data for the LMC at the time of their study. Based on their chemical abundances, Nidever2019 concluded that the early evolution of the MCs was characterized by low star formation efficiency (gas mass converted into stellar mass).

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as the SMC’s interacting dwarf-galaxy companion, giving its distance, role in Magellanic System simulations, and assumed mass relative to the SMC.

quotes.quote: Magellanic Clouds -- galaxies: interactions -- proper motions -- stars: kinematics and dynamics The Magellanic System, one of the nearest examples of an interacting system of galaxies, comprises two dwarf galaxies, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), a bridge of gas and stars connecting these galaxies known as the Magellanic Bridge (MB), a leading stream of gas known as the Leading Arm (LA) and a trailing stream of gas known as the Magellanic Stream (MS). The Magellanic Clouds (MCs) are located at a distance of 50 2 kpc (LMC -- degrijs2014lmc ) and 62 1 kpc (SMC -- degrijs2015smc ). The MB, MS and the LA are prominent features in HI maps putman2003 . Simulations of the Magellanic System Besla2012,Diaz2012 , based on the revised proper motion estimates of the MCs ( kallivayalil2006,vieira2010 ) are able to explain the formation of many of the observed gaseous features around the MCs as a result of their mutual interactions.

In the N-body simulation of the Magellanic System by the LMC is treated as a point mass and the SMC is represented as a multi-component system composed of an exponential disc (truncation radius = 5 kpc and disc scale-length = 1 kpc), a central spheroid and a dark matter halo. The authors consider three models for the central spheroidal component of the SMC and found that an extended spheroid (truncation radius = 7.5 kpc and scale-length = 1.5 kpc) best reproduces the observed features. The assumed total mass of the LMC and the SMC are 10 ^ 10 M _ and 3 10 ^ 9 M _ respectively.

sources.summary: The paper uses the Large Magellanic Cloud as a local comparison for RR Lyrae mapping, noting its RR Lyrae-traced stellar halo and less concentrated RR Lyrae distribution than the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: In particular, these studies cannot be done in other local spiral galaxies like M31 and M33 because they are far enough away that crowding does not allow us to reach the innermost RRL variables. Other sizeable local galaxies where the RRL can be reasonably mapped all the way to their centres are the Sgr dwarf and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). On the one hand, the LMC has a stellar halo as mapped by the RRL density distribution Drake13 and their kinematics ( Minniti03 , and Borissova04 ). On the other hand, the Sgr dwarf galaxy is currently in an advanced state of disruption (e.g. Majewski03 , and Hasselquist18 ), with a sizeable fraction of its RRL lost in the tails Ibata20 . However, neither of these galaxies have been reported to have a very concentrated RRL distribution.

Given that the Local Group of spirals M31 and M33 are inaccessible, other galaxies where a proper RRL census can be traced is within lower luminosity galaxies of the Local Group, such as the Sgr dwarf galaxy and the LMC. Nonetheless, their RRL distributions do not appear to be as concentrated as that in the MW.

sources.summary: The LMC is analyzed with adopted distance, orientation, metallicity, MAGMA CO coverage, Spitzer 24-micron imaging, and SHASSA H-alpha data.

quotes.quote: LMC. We adopt a distance of 50 kpc pietrzynski19 , an inclination of 22.0 ^ , and a position angle of 168.0 ^ kim98 . The adopted metallicity at the centre of the LMC is 12+log(O/H)_ 0 =8.35 0.03 , with a radial gradient of 0.0105 0.0105 \ dex kpc ^ 1 , as measured by toribio17 using a direct measurement of electron temperature ( T_ e ) from spectra of \ regions (direct T_ e based method). We employ the data presented in the third data release of the Magellanic Mopra Assessment MAGMA; wong11, wong17 to trace molecular gas. MAGMA is a CO mapping survey of the LMC and SMC using the Mopra 22-m Telescope at the Australia Telescope National Facility. For the LMC, the observations were conducted as a follow-up study of the NANTEN survey fukui08 by targeting a subset of previously identified molecular clouds ( 160 out of 272 clouds), with an improved resolution in order to resolve the GMCs ( 11 pc).

sources.summary: The historical review recounts early studies of the Large Magellanic Cloud, including a flat rotating disk model, Milky Way interaction, and H I survey results.

quotes.quote: He is also known for proposing a flat, rotating disk model for the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) 1955AJ.....60..126D , which was confirmed by Kerr, McGee and others at the Radiophysics Lab with 21-cm observations. Combining their ideas and results, 1955AuJPh...8..508K was the first extragalactic 21 cm astronomy article. 1955AJ.....60..126D also advocated that there had been a past interaction between the Milky Way and the LMC, a result later supported by Kerr's observations of our galaxy's disturbed gas disc 1957AJ.....62...93K .

He is known for his contribution to our knowledge of the Milky Way's chemical abundance gradient 1983MNRAS.204...53S , and his detailed HI survey 1966AuJPh..19..343M which revealed the distribution and motion of hydrogen gas in the LMC, including a primitive spiral pattern and local fuel supply for the stellar nurseries scattered around the galaxy The survey was so important that it was repeated at Parkes in 1984 1984AA...137..343R . . Miss Janice A. \ Milton (later Mrs J.A. \ Weedon) and McGee also published a survey of neutral hydrogen across the southern sky, in addition to their focus on the LMC, which was repeated with the Parkes telescope when its instrumentation improved.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a major ongoing Milky Way accretion, expected to bring dwarf satellites and used as a fiducial recent massive progenitor.

quotes.quote: The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), currently believed to be on its first infall into the MW Besla2010 , is expected to have contributed a number of dwarf galaxies to the Galaxy's existing satellite population e.g. Deason2015 . A number of ultra-faint dwarf galaxies have been already spatially and kinematically associated with the LMC e.g. Sales2011,Sales2017,Kallivayalil2018 , while there are hints that a few classical dwarfs may also have been accreted with a more massive LMC (e.g. Fornax and Carina, Pardy2019,Erkal2019 , although see Patel2020 for a different view).
There is firm evidence that the MW has suffered at least two large accretions, the LMC and Gaia-Enceladus Helmi2018 .

The expected crossing-time of the LMC into the virial halo of the MW depends on their assumed masses, and is about 1.5-3 Gyr Kallivayalil2013 . The accretion of Gaia-Enceladus is estimated to be about 10-11 Gyr Helmi2018 . On the other hand, M31's large accreted progenitor is expected to have entered the virial halo about 5-6 Gyr ago (see Appendix Appendix:1 ). We adopt the following accretion times for the MW: 2.5 and 10.5 Gyr for the LMC and Gaia-Enceladus respectively.

sources.summary: The review describes the LMC as a nearby face-on, half-solar-metallicity galaxy with high gas-to-dust ratio, active star-forming regions, and detected complex molecules in hot cores.

quotes.quote: In the vicinity, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), located at about 50 kpc, is a galaxy that is seen nearly face-on with an inclination angle of 35 ^ . The metallicity in the LMC is Z 0.5 Z _ keller06 , and the gas-to-dust ratio is a factor of 4 higher than in the Milky Way. This galaxy has many active star-forming and HII regions (e.g. paron16,och17 ). Therefore, the LMC is a unique laboratory for studying the effects of metallicity on molecular gas and star formation at high spatial resolution in an extragalactic medium. Indeed, several complex molecules have been detected towards hot cores in the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper identifies the LMC as a face-on nearby star-forming galaxy where CTA can resolve diffuse emission, individual objects, and cosmic-ray transport outside the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: In addition, a survey of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) will provide a face-on view of an entire star-forming galaxy, resolving regions down to 20 pc in size. This will allow to map the diffuse LMC emission as well as individual objects, thus providing key information on relativistic particle transport in a galaxy different from the Milky Way.

On the scale of individual galaxies, the LMC provides a unique target hosting a number of extraordinary objects, including 30 Doradus - the most active star-forming region in the local group of galaxies, the super star cluster R136, supernova SN1987A and the 30 Dor C superbubble. It is one of the nearest star-forming galaxies, with one-tenth of the star formation rate of the Milky Way distributed in only two percent of its volume. Since it is observed face-on, at high Galactic latitudes, observations of the LMC will provide a well-resolved global view of a star-forming galaxy at very high energies, allowing to study the transport of CRs from their release into the interstellar medium to their escape from the system.

sources.summary: The paper models the infall of a massive LMC, showing it changes Local Group dwarf orbits and identifying likely, possible, and recently captured LMC satellites.

quotes.quote: The bulk motions are integrated in three gravitational potentials: two where the Milky Way is treated in isolation and has a mass 0.9 1.6 10^ 12 M _ and the time-varying potential by Vasiliev et al. (2021), which includes the infall of a massive Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC).

The inclusion of the infall of a massive LMC significantly modifies the orbital trajectories of the objects, with respect to orbit integration in static Milky Way-only potentials, and leads to 6 galaxies being likely associated to the LMC, 3 possibly associated and 1 recently captured object.

We also carried out an analysis aimed at identifying which ones of the galaxies surrounding the MW might have been/are physically linked to the LMC. We first identify what galaxies have at least an orbit that brings them within 60 kpc from the LMC, and then look at their total velocity and position as compared to the escape velocity curve of the LMC as a function of time. In the hypothesis that the gravitational potential and time evolution of the MW + LMC system is well represented by the Vasiliev_21 model, we find 6 systems that are highly likely to have been satellites of the LMC (Carina II, Carina III, Horologium I, Hydrus I, Phoenix II, Reticulum II), 3 that might have been potentially associated as satellites (Horologium II, Tucana IV, Carina) and one that seems to have been recently captured (Grus II).

sources.summary: The paper models the Large Magellanic Cloud losing stellar halo material to Milky Way tides, predicts a northern leading stream, and identifies RR Lyrae candidates far from the LMC.

quotes.quote: We examine whether the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is currently losing its stellar halo to Milky Way (MW) tides. We present a live N body model for the ongoing MW-LMC interaction that predicts a prominent stream of stars tidally stripped from the progenitor LMC. We use this model to define a strategy to search for stripped material in kinematic space.

Using a sample of RR Lyrae stars with Gaia EDR3 astrometry we show that the LMC stellar halo in the Southern Galactic hemisphere extends at least out to 30^ from the galaxy centre. In addition, several leading arm candidates are found in the Northern hemisphere as far above the disc plane as b=+34^ (at 68 ^ from the LMC).

sources.summary: The paper frames the LMC as the nearest low-metallicity star-forming galaxy, with stronger FUV radiation than the Milky Way and prior evidence for warm molecular gas in its star-forming regions.

quotes.quote: The Large Magellanic Cloud is the closest star-forming galaxy to our Milky Way ( 50 kpc, e.g., Pietrzynski2013,Pietrzynski2019 ). In the LMC, the far-ultraviolet (FUV) radiation field is stronger than in the Milky Way while metallicities are lower Westerlund1990,Rolleston2002 . The LMC provides an ideal laboratory for studying star formation, particularly massive star formation associated with its numerous stellar clusters in such an active low-metallicity galaxy.

Previous observations of CO J = 1--0 and 3--2 show that the higher gas temperatures in low/moderate density regions ( n (H _2 ) a few 10 ^3 cm ^ 3 in most cases) are correlated with stronger H flux in giant molecular clouds of the LMC Minamidani2008,Minamidani2011 . Further observations of CO J = 4--3 to 12--11 indicate that the gas temperatures are high ( T_ kin > 150 K) at densities 10 ^3 cm ^ 3 in active star-forming regions of the LMC Lee2016 .

sources.summary: The paper studies extinction in the Large Magellanic Cloud bar, finding variable but uniform reddening properties, a grey optical component from big grains, and links to intense star formation.

quotes.quote: We report on the extinction properties in the fields around the clusters NGC 1854, NGC 1856, and NGC 1858 in the bar of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). The colour--magnitude diagrams of the stars in all these regions show an elongated red giant clump (RC) that reveals a variable amount of extinction across these fields, ranging from A_V 0.2 to A_V 1.9 , including Galactic foreground extinction. The extinction properties nonetheless are remarkably uniform. The slope of the reddening vectors measured in the ( V-I , V ) and ( B-I , B ) colour--magnitude planes is fully in line with the A_V/E(B-V) 5.5 value found in the outskirts of 30 Dor. This indicates the presence of an additional grey extinction component in the optical requiring big grains to be about twice as abundant as in the diffuse Galactic interstellar medium (ISM).

sources.summary: The paper uses LMC europium-to-magnesium measurements and long star-formation duration as comparison data for delayed neutron-star-merger enrichment models.

quotes.quote: In addition to the [ Eu/Mg ] data we have measured for Kraken and GSE, we also compare our models qualitatively against [ Eu/Mg ] measured for dwarf galaxies of comparable mass (LMC, Fornax, and Sgr). For the LMC we draw on vanderswaelmen13 who measured [ Eu/Mg ] for 94 stars spanning [ Fe/H ] of -0.9 to -0.4 (10 ^ th and 90 ^ th percentiles, median [ Fe/H ] of 0.66^ +0.02 _ 0.02 ).

Star-formation durations for these galaxies (all 10 Gyrs) are sourced from Weisz14sfh - in particular, we adopt their t_ 90 which is the time taken to form 90 of the stellar mass. These galaxies do not have simple top-hat star-formation histories, their abundances are measured on different scales, and [ Eu/Mg ] is measured mostly at the metal-rich end of these galaxies, i.e., they are probing the [ Eu/Mg ] at the very end of the SFH and not the median [ Eu/Mg ] across the entire SFH. Galaxies like the LMC, Fornax, and Sgr have much higher [ Eu/Mg ] at fixed metallicity compared to the MW.

sources.summary: The paper uses likely LMC-associated dwarfs to constrain the LMC virial velocity, contrasting cutoff and power-law satellite models and disfavoring a 100 km/s halo.

quotes.quote: These authors use kinematic information to identify LMC-associated dwarfs; their list of most likely LMC satellites include 7 satellites: the SMC, Hydrus 1, Horologium 1, Carina 3, Tucana 4, Reticulum 2, and Phoenix 2 (dashed black line). A less likely, but still plausible association is also ascribed to Carina, Horologium 2, Grus 2, and Fornax, bringing the total to 11 (solid black line). In the context of the ``cutoff'' model, these numbers seem to rule out a virial velocity as low as 50 km/s (bottom blue curve), and suggest a virial velocity a little below 100 km/s (top blue curve). In contrast, a virial velocity near the lower bound would be favoured in the case of the ``power-law'' model. An LMC halo as massive as 100 km/s seem quite inconsistent with the data in this case.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as the interacting companion whose tidal force on the SMC about 2 Gyr ago enhances massive GMC and star-cluster formation.

quotes.quote: Here we investigate whether massive SCs with initial masses more than 10^5 M _ can be formed during tidal interaction of the SMC with the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) about 2 Gyr ago, based on our new simulations, which include molecular hydrogen formation on dust grains and SC formation within giant molecular clouds (GMCs). We find that the formation of GMCs with masses more than 10^5 M _ can be dramatically enhanced due to the tidal force of the LMC-SMC interaction.

The Large and Small Magellanic Clouds, due to their proximity to our Galaxy, offer a unique opportunity to examine the roles of their tidal interactions in the formation and evolution of various stellar and gaseous objects (e.g., K11_Westerlund1997 ; K43_DOnghia_Fox2016 ). The two irregular dwarf galaxies are interacting with the Galaxy, which could have influenced the evolution of these three systems (e.g., 78Murai_Fujimoto1980 ; K12_Weinberg2000 , 69Yoshizawa_Noguchi2003 ; K13_Besla_et_al2012 ; K3_Diaz_Bekki2012 ). Two of the most recent LMC-SMC interactions that possibly occurred 0.2 , and 2 Gyr ago (e.g., 69Yoshizawa_Noguchi2003 ; 20Harris_Zaritsky2004 ; 1Rafelski_Zaritsky2005 ; 29Bekki_Chiba2007 ; 17Bekki_Chiba2009 ; 11Bitsakis_et_al2018 ; 39Nayak_et_al2018 ) have been investigated by astronomers in the contexts of the formation of the Magellanic Stream ( K15_Gardiner_Noguchi1996 ; 67Sawa_Fujimoto_Kumai1999 ; 89Pardy_DOnghia_Fox2018 ), its leading arms ( M6_Cullinane_et_al2021 ) and the Bridge ( 24Zivick_et_al2019 ), as well as the chemical and dynamical evolution of the Clouds ( M8_Bekki_Chiba2005 ; M9_Patel_et_al2020 ).

sources.summary: The paper places the radio ring near the LMC, argues for an intergalactic SNR from an LMC-outskirts star, and cites LMC outflows and envelope evidence.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of , a low-surface brightness radio ring, lying between the Galactic Plane and the LMC .

We argue that the most plausible explanation for \ is an intergalactic supernova remnant due to a star that resided in the LMC outskirts that had undergone a single-degenerate type Ia supernova, and we are seeing its remnant expand into a rarefied, intergalactic environment.

There is clear observational evidence of local outflows emanating from supergiant shells in the LMC and a trailing filament of \ gas mainly originating from the 30-Doradus region as well as the Leading Arm connection. Indeed, looking in the Parkes Galactic All-Sky Survey (GASS/HI4PI; angular resolution is 16.2 arcmin; ) \ image (Fig. fig:9 ), we can see that the LMC \ envelope covers a much larger area than the optical (and radio-continuum) emission, including the location of this new object that is within the \ envelope of the LMC . Evidence and modelling for the presence of such interaction waves and star formation has been presented by 2016ApJ...825...20B and conroy .

sources.summary: The paper compares the LMC star formation history with the SMC, finding synchronized enhancements over 3.5 Gyr and linking them to interactions shaping the LMC spiral arm.

quotes.quote: We compare these to the SFH of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) finding unequivocal synchronicity, with both galaxies displaying similar periods of enhanced star formation over the past 3.5 Gyr. The parallelism between their SFHs indicates that tidal interactions between the MCs have recurrently played an important role in their evolution for at least the last 3.5 Gyr, tidally truncating the SMC and shaping the LMC's spiral arm. We show, for the first time, an SMC-LMC correlated SFH at recent times in which enhancements of star formation are localised in the northern spiral arm of the LMC, and globally across the SMC.

The LMC's SFH presents multiple episodes of star formation with several recent enhancements ( Harris2009 ; Monteagudo2018 ; Ruiz-Lara2020b ; Mazzi2021 ) that are possibly the products of interactions. Also, the age distribution of the LMC's cluster population seems to correlate with its global SFH, with two major periods of star and cluster formation, one at old ages ( 12-13.7 Gyr ago) and another in the past 3 Gyr e.g.

sources.summary: The paper uses the Magellanic Clouds to build dwarf-galaxy scattering models, citing their pulsar DM distribution, uncertain scattering attribution, and analytic LMC and SMC density models.

quotes.quote: We base our density model for dwarf galaxies on the Magellanic Clouds, the only two other galaxies besides the Milky Way with observed pulsar populations. The observed DM distribution for the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC/SMC) spans 45 to 273 pc cm ^ 3 2013MNRAS.433..138R, 2019MNRAS.487.4332T , exceeding the predicted DM contributions of dwarf galaxies in . Measured scattering has been reported for several pulsars in the LMC and SMC. In most cases the scattering time is inferred from the scintillation bandwidth and is between 0.1 and 1 s at 1 GHz 2022MNRAS.509.5209J , but it is unclear whether this scattering can be attributed to the Magellanic Clouds or to the Milky Way ISM. PSR B0540 69 has substantial scattering that is likely associated with the pulsar's supernova remnant 2003ApJ...590L..95J,2021MNRAS.505.4468G . The scattering observed from two FRBs residing in dwarf galaxies at z 0.2 , FRB 121102 and FRB 20190520B, suggests that can range from 0.1 to 2 (pc ^ 2 km) ^ 1/3 in these environments ( 2021arXiv210801172C, 2022arXiv220213458O ). provide analytic density models for the LMC and SMC that were fit to their observed DM distributions.

sources.summary: The LMC is included as a massive gravitational perturber whose influence changes Pegasus III and Pisces II pericenter timing and distance, with tested masses giving similar histories.

quotes.quote: However, when the influence of the LMC is included, the satellites complete a pericentric approach around the MW at 1.5 Gyr ago in both MW mass models at distances of 50--90 kpc. Thus, the influence of the LMC significantly increases the distance at pericenter about the MW and changes the timing at which the pericenter occurs. This effect is two-fold as it is caused by the gravitational influence of the LMC itself in addition to the LMC causing the MW's center of mass to move in response to the passage of the LMC. We also test LMC masses in the range 8-- 25 10^ 10  M _ and find no significant differences in the resulting orbital histories. These results are in contrast to the role the LMC plays on the orbit of Crater 2, for example, where including the LMC plunges Crater 2 further into the halo of the MW, providing a possible explanation for its puffed up morphology Torrealba2016,Caldwell2017,Fritz2018,Fu2019,Ji2021 .

sources.summary: The paper models LMC globular-cluster escape, arguing its larger initial orbital radius delayed escape and estimating that about four LMC clusters have already escaped.

quotes.quote: The average escape time for the LMC and SMC is 7.9 and 6.5 respectively. Given that the initial orbital radii of the LMC and SMC were larger (due to dynamical friction), their GCs did not initially feel a strong tidal field from the MW. As a result, the GCs could escape from the LMC and SMC only after a long time. Today, LMC and SMC have 19 and 8 GCs, respectively Law2010 . According to the escape fractions that we have obtained, we expect about 4 GCs of the LMC and 2 GCs of the SMC to have already escaped from their host galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper compares Large Magellanic Cloud ISM structure with simulations, finding most regions inconsistent with feedback-only driving and requiring another large-scale turbulence driver.

quotes.quote: A systematic comparison with the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is then performed. Only 1 out of 25 regions has a coherent power spectrum which is consistent with the feedback-only simulation. A detailed study of the turn-over scale leads us to conclude that regular stellar feedback is not enough to explain the observed ISM structure on scales larger than 60 pc. Extreme feedback in the form of supergiant shells likely plays an important role but cannot explain all the regions of the LMC. If we assume ISM structure is generated by turbulence, another large scale driving mechanism is needed to explain the entirety of the observations.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a gas-rich dwarf model galaxy with evidence for outflows and only minor K-S-law shifts in its confinement behavior.

quotes.quote: The five galaxies we have chosen are the Milky Way (a large, older, spiral galaxy), M82 (a starburst galaxy), the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) (a dwarf galaxy), NGC 4449 (a dwarf starburst galaxy), and DRC-8 (a massive, young, starbursting galaxy). It is important to note that winds have been observed for M82 OconnellM82Central1978, StricklandM82Wind2009 and NGC 4449 McQuinnDwarfStarburst2019 . There is also evidence for outflows from the LMC StaveleySmithLMCHI2003, BargerLMCWind2016 and some analyses have interpreted the observation of soft, diffuse X-ray emission in some regions of the Milky Way as evidence for a wind everettwinds2008 .

The only galaxies that exhibit only minor shifts based on the K-S Law are the LMC and M82. For the LMC, we can see when comparing Figure fig:CombinedUc0VaryPlotsSSL to Figure fig:CombinedUc0VaryPlotsSSL_LowerSFR that its confined radii and mass are slightly larger for the final value of U_ c0 when = 1.4 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies the LMC as currently on first infall into the Milky Way, massive enough to host satellites, and responsible for a recent accretion-rate peak.

quotes.quote: This second peak is due to the accretion of the LMC, which is currently at its first infall onto the MW e.g. Besla2007,Cautun2019LMC . Due to its relatively large mass, the LMC also hosts its own satellite galaxies e.g. Jethwa2016,Patel2020LMCSMC . It is therefore a reassuring sight that our results shows a peak in infall probability around the 1.5 Gyr mark, which is when the LMC would have entered the MW halo (see Figure fig:tinf_results_1 ), together with its satellites.

For many of the dwarfs that have been proposed as LMC satellites we find likelihoods very similar to that of the LMC even though our Galactic model does not include a massive LMC component. This find illustrates the robustness of our results and that neglecting the LMC potential is a reasonable first approximation.

sources.summary: The paper describes the LMC as an ideal nearby laboratory for hot-ISM studies, with a large SNR sample, stellar-mass comparison to the Milky Way, and many evolved Sedov-phase remnants.

quotes.quote: The two largest satellite galaxies of the Milky Way, Large and Small Magellanic Cloud (LMC, SMC), with their low foreground absorption and small distances of 50 kpc and 60 kpc, respectively, are the ideal laboratories for exploring the global structures of the ISM in a galaxy. Studies of different types of SNRs and a systematic study of the SNR populations in the LMC and SMC have been performed in radio, optical, and X-rays e.g., 2016A&A...585A.162M,2017ApJS..230....2B,2021MNRAS.500.2336Y , yielding 60 confirmed SNRs in the LMC and many more candidates. With stellar masses of 2.7 10^ 9 M_ 2006lgal.symp...47V and 6 10^ 10 M_ 2015ApJ...806...96L in the LMC and the Milky Way, respectively, one would expect about one order of magnitude higher number of SNRs in the Milky Way.

Compared to 300 known SNRs and candidates in the Milky Way 2019JApA...40...36G , the sample in the LMC is more complete. Many of the SNRs were found to be evolved SNRs in the Sedov phase of evolution, in which they are expanding adiabatically.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a systematic source for Milky Way mass work: its mass causes outer-halo sloshing, velocity shifts, and biased mass estimates.

quotes.quote: Petersen2021 and Erkal2021 have used distant halo stars to measure the present-day reflex (or sloshing) motion of the stellar halo which is induced by the LMC. The LMC's mass induces systematic motions in the outer halo, affecting the measured velocities in Galactic longitude V_l (and in the vertical direction, V_Z ), with the largest influence occurring for >50 kpc. Sloshing of the halo by the LMC has been examined using simulations Gomez2015, Erkal2020b, Garavito-Camargo2019, Cunningham2020,Petersen2020, Wang_Jianling2022 . Specifically, Erkal2020b run Milky Way models to quantify the bias introduced in mass determinations of the Milky Way for this effect, since the assumption of equilibrium dynamics is broken. This bias can cause an overestimation of the Milky Way mass by 25 percent.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Gaia EDR3 red clump stars across the LMC disk, detecting a northeastern outer warp matching the southwestern warp direction and interpreting the disk as an asymmetric U-shaped warp.

quotes.quote: A recent study identified a stellar warp in the outer regions of the south-western (SW) disk of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and suggested that it might have originated due to the tidal interaction between the LMC and the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC). Due to the limited spatial coverage of the data, the authors could not investigate the counterpart of this warp in the north-eastern (NE) region, which is essential to understanding the global shape, nature, and origin of the outer LMC warp. In this work, we study the structure of the LMC disk using data on red clump stars from the Gaia Early Data Release 3 (EDR3), which cover the entire Magellanic system. We detected a warp in the NE outer LMC disk which is deviated from the disk plane in the same direction as that of the SW outer warp, but with a lower amplitude. This suggests that the outer LMC disk has an asymmetric stellar warp, which is likely to be a U-shaped warp.

sources.summary: The paper uses the LMC as a benchmark Local Group galaxy, citing its complete evolved-star sample and CMD features to classify AGB/RSG populations and calibrate C/M analysis.

quotes.quote: The Magellanic Clouds With a distance much shorter than the other twelve galaxies, the LMC and the SMC locate at 49.59 0.09 (stat.) 0.54 (syst.) kpc pietrzynski2019distance and 62.44 kpc 0.47 (stat.) 0.81 (syst.) kpc graczyk2020distance , respectively. They are the two closest galaxies in our sample and their JHK magnitudes are taken from the Two Micron All-Sky Survey (2MASS) skrutskie2006two .

With a short distance to the Milky Way, the LMC has the most complete and best-measured sample of evolved stars and it is commonly selected as the calibrator of stellar models pastorelli2019constraining,pastorelli2020constraining . There are 198,548 sources with _ JHK 0.05 mag in the final sample. The (J-K)_0/K_0 diagram of these sources in Figure fig1 clearly illustrates various types of evolved stars above TRGB: RSGs brighter and bluer than TRGB, O-AGBs brighter and redder than TRGB, C-AGBs even redder than O-AGBs that extend out like a tail marigo2003red at K 11 mag, and the most luminous sources are RSGs and TP-AGB stars ( K_0 < 10 mag).

sources.summary: The paper models the Orphan-Chenab stream with a moving extended LMC, constraining its mass profile, halo extent, past trajectory, dynamical friction, and stream perturbations.

quotes.quote: We model the 6-D stream track by a Lagrange-point stripping method with a flexible MW potential in the presence of a moving extended Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). This allows us to constrain the mass profile of the MW within the distance range 15.6 < < 55.5 kpc, with the best measured enclosed mass of (2.85 0.1) 10^ 11 within 32.4 kpc. Our stream measurements are highly sensitive to the LMC mass profile with the most precise measurement of its enclosed mass made at 32.8 kpc, (7.02 0.9) 10^ 10 M _ . We also detect that the LMC dark matter halo extends to at least 53 kpc. The fitting of the OC stream allows us to constrain the past LMC trajectory and the degree of dynamical friction it experienced. We demonstrate that the stars in the OC stream show large energy and angular momentum spreads caused by LMC perturbation.

sources.summary: The paper discusses the LMC as massive enough to displace the Milky Way, affect timing-argument Local Group masses, and alter MW-M31 dynamical modeling.

quotes.quote: Since then several studies have taken the TA one step further by including the influence of the LMC given its substantial mass at least 10 the mass of the MW; penarrubia16, benisty22, chamberlain22 . The LMC is expected to displace the MW's disk and inner halo over time, and impart a reflex motion on the MW e.g. gomez15, garavitoc19, petersen20, cunningham20, gc21 . Furthermore, recently measured the motion of the inner MW halo relative to the outer halo, or ``travel velocity", confirming that the gravitational influence of the LMC is critical to the dynamics of the LG.

By modeling the three galaxies and other Local Volume galaxies as a dynamic system, they simultaneously fit for distances and velocities of all galaxies while leaving the LMC's mass as a free parameter in a Bayesian fashion. In doing so, individual masses are derived for each of the MW, M31 (see Fig. fig:litmasses ), the LMC, and the total LG mass for which they find 2.64^ +0.42 _ 0.38 10^ 12 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper uses LMC RR Lyrae photometry to validate empirical PLZ/PWZ relations, deriving near-infrared distance moduli and attributing offsets and scatter to calibration, reddening, metallicity, and geometry uncertainties.

quotes.quote: To validate the accuracy of our empirically derived PLZ and PWZ relations, we determined a distance to the LMC using available NIR photometry in the literature. We use 18500 RR Lyrae stars (13611 RRab, 4848 RRc) from cusano2021 with JK_s photometry from the VMC survey together with their photometric metallicity and reddening estimates. Our empirical calibrations result in an average distance modulus to the LMC of 18.54 0.13 , 18.57 0.12 , and 18.60 0.15 mag in K_s band using the whole sample, RRab, and RRc+RRd stars, respectively.

When using theoretical relations of marconi2015 , we obtained a distance of 18.60 0.13 , and 18.59 0.15 mag for all/RRab and RRc+RRd samples, respectively, which reflects the difference in the slopes and zero-points between empirical and theoretical calibrations.
