sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a key Milky Way satellite with precise proper motion, fast pericentric motion, and an orbital pole close to the concentrated satellite-pole distribution.

quotes.quote: For the LMC and Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) we adopt the most recent PM measurements by 2013ApJ...764..161K , based on a 7 yr baseline covered by three epochs of Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) data. The resulting PMs are very precise and the uncertainties are dominated by limitations in our understanding of the internal kinematics and geometry of the LMC and SMC, according to 2013ApJ...764..161K .
The two innermost satellite galaxies (Sagittarius and the LMC) are significantly faster, but they are close to the pericentre of their orbit.

The orbital pole of the LMC, as well as the normal to the plane containing the Magellanic Stream and the VPOS-3, the plane fitted to all known MW satellites except three outliers, all are very close to the centre of the concentrated orbital pole distribution.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a massive, out-of-equilibrium Milky Way satellite whose HST proper motions enter orbit integrations; it adopts a 3e10 solar-mass value for the LMC.

quotes.quote: HST proper-motion measurements (Kallivayallil et al. 2006, Kallivayallil et al. 2013, henceforth K13) of the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC, SMC) have been used to derive past orbits of these two satellites (Besla et al. 2007, henceforth B07), and of Leo I (Sohn et al. 2013; Boylan-Kolchin et al. 2013). Masses of dwarf galaxies remain however highly uncertain. This is particularly true of the most massive ones (Sgr, LMC, SMC) that are significantly out of equilibrium (Walker et al. 2009; McConnachie 2012).

Given the measured 3d position and velocity, ( x , v ) , derived from HST proper motions of the the Sgr dwarf (Pryor et al. 2010) and the LMC and SMC (K13), we integrate the equations of motion backwards in time as in prior work (B07).
For the LMC, we employ the mass most directly motivated by imaging surveys (Saha et al. 2010), which gives 3 10^ 10 M_ for the LMC, and we adopt 3 10^ 9 M_ for the SMC.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as one member of the interacting Magellanic pair and finds its red-clump population extends 15 degrees from the center, overlapping the SMC population in the Bridge.

quotes.quote: The Magellanic Clouds (MCs) comprise of two galaxies: the Large and the Small Magellanic Cloud (LMC and SMC, respectively), and are the closest to the Milky Way (MW) pair of interacting galaxies.
Another possibility is that the LMC and SMC have become an interacting pair only recently, with a first close encounter 2 Gyr ago and a second 250 Myr ago.

We see that the RC stars are present as far as 15 deg from the LMC center and 9 deg from the SMC center, although this does not yet mean that the RC populations of the two galaxies overlap, since both the LMC and the SMC ellipses have major axes pointing away from the main MBR. But if we consider subfields only in the conservative Bridge region between the Clouds ( 75^ < <-70^ ), which are plotted with black crosses, we see no definite distinction as where the LMC population ends and the SMC population begins, which shows that the RC populations of the two galaxies overlap, as inferred from the density map (Figure fig:maps_rc ).

sources.summary: The paper models the LMC in Magellanic-system simulations, varying its proper motion and matching its rotation, mass, and encounters with the SMC.

quotes.quote: Using proper motion measurements from the Hubble Space Telescope, show that the Large Magellanic Cloud (hereafter LMC) is more likely on its first passage around the Milky Way.

Traces of these encounters exist in the recent star formation history (SFH) of the Clouds, as strong tidal interactions between LMC and SMC might have caused episodic star bursts in both galaxies 2005MNRAS.356..680B,2005MNRAS.358.1215P .

As shown in the left panel of Figure fig:MCcurve , the total circular velocity of the initial LMC peaks at 105 km/s (black solid line), which is consistent with the recent estimation by who quote the rotation curve of the LMC to be 91 18 km s^ 1 2014ApJ...781..121V . The initial mass of LMC is chosen so that the total mass within 9 kpc is 19 10^9 M _ ; whereas the right panel shows the rotation curve of the initial SMC.

sources.summary: The paper includes the Large Magellanic Cloud as a nearby dark-matter halo target with high Mdm/d2 and notes southern neutrino telescopes would help observe it.

quotes.quote: In addition, nearby galaxies M31 , Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and Small Magellanic Cloud are considered. The signal is stronger for objects with larger M_ dm /d^2 , where M_ dm is the dark matter halo mass and d is the distance. Virgo, Fornax, M31, and LMC are of particular interest, and they have M_ dm /d^2 a few 10 ^ 13 M_ Mpc ^ 2 .
Note, however, that a proposed km ^3 scale neutrino telescope like KM3Net in the Mediterranean Sea should be helpful for neutrino observations from Fornax and LMC in the Southern Hemisphere.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as one of the few gas-rich, star-forming Local Group satellites and uses its likely first-infall status to constrain environmental quenching timescales.

quotes.quote: This trend has just a few exceptions: 4 gas-rich, star-forming galaxies persist within the halos of the MW (the LMC and SMC) and M31 (LGS 3 and IC 10), and 4 - 5 quiescent, gas-poor galaxies reside well beyond of either the MW or M31: Cetus Lewis2007 , Tucana Fraternali2009 , KKR 25 Makarov2012 , KKs 3 Karachentsev2015 , and possibly Andromeda XVIII, though Cetus and Tucana may have orbited within the MW halo Teyssier2012 .

By this definition, the only star-forming, gas-rich satellites are: LMC ( =1.5 10^9 , / 0.3 ) and SMC ( =4.6 10^8 , / 1 ) around the MW, LGS 3 ( =9.6 10^5 , / 0.4 ) and IC 10 ( =9 10^7 , / 0.6 ) around M31.

The 3-D orbital velocity measured for the LMC/SMC strongly suggests that they are experiencing first infall and crossed inside of the MW 2 ago Kallivayalil2013 . Given that the LMC and SMC remain star-forming, this places a lower limit to their quenching timescale (gray triangle), consistent with our statistical timescales.

sources.summary: The paper uses the LMC to anchor Stream modeling, citing its HST proper-motion orbital velocity, roughly 50 kpc distance, LMC-pulsar dispersion measures, and disc-stripping constraints on halo density.

quotes.quote: The proper motion of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) has recently been measured using HST data, yielding an orbital velocity v_ lmc = 321 24 kal13a .
The distance to the nearest point of the MS is believed to be d 55 , which is the average distance to the LMC 50 ; wal12a , and the SMC 60 ; gra14a .

and10a infer a range for the mean halo density of n (6 - 10) 10^ 4 out to the LMC ( d 50 ) based on dispersion measures of LMC pulsars. However, the stripping of the LMC's disc requires a somewhat lower value of n(48.2 3 ) = (1.1 0.44) 10^ 4 sal15a .

sources.summary: The paper constrains the LMC total mass, arguing for an extended dark halo, first pericentric passage, and significant perturbations to Local Group timing estimates.

quotes.quote: This paper explores the effect of the LMC on the mass estimates obtained from the timing argument. We show that accounting for the presence of the LMC systematically lowers the Local Group mass ( M_ ) derived from the relative motion of the Milky Way--Andromeda pair. We find a (total) LMC mass M_ =0.25_ 0.08 ^ +0.09 10^ 12 M_ , which is indicative of an extended dark matter halo and supports the scenario where this galaxy is just past its first pericentric approach.

With a circular velocity curve that peaks at v_ max 90 and remains flat out to 8.7 (van der Marel Kallivayalil 2014) the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC hereinafter) has a (minimum) bound mass M_ (<8.7 )= 1.7 10^ 10 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper derives new first-overtone Cepheid PL relations for the Large Magellanic Cloud, compares them with SMC relations, and estimates a 0.49 mag relative distance modulus.

quotes.quote: We also derive new optical and mid-infrared band PL relations for first-overtone mode Cepheids in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). We compare multi-band PL relations for first-overtone mode Cepheids in the Magellanic Clouds and find a significant difference in the slope of the V band PL relations but not for I band PL relations. The slope of PL relations are found to be consistent in most of the infrared bands. A relative distance modulus of =0.49 0.02 mag between the two clouds is estimated using multi-band PL relations for the first-overtone mode Cepheids in the SMC and LMC.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes the LMC molecular ISM at 60 pc, describing it as a Local Group dwarf spiral whose gas has low line widths, a bright molecular ridge, and scale-dependent boundedness.

quotes.quote: The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and M33 are both Local Group dwarf spiral galaxies, which have weaker stellar potential wells than large spirals and where the molecular gas exists within a dominant atomic gas reservoir. Because of their proximity, these have been the targets of GMC studies for more than two decades e.g., WILSON90,FUKUI99,ROSOLOWSKY03,HUGHES10 .
In the LMC, the extension to high I is due to the molecular ridge on the eastern edge of the galaxy (south of 30 Doradus), which contains the brightest emission in the galaxy.
Line Width and Mach Number: The average line width varies from _ 60pc _ 500pc 3.5 km s ^ 1 in the LMC to 4 5 km s ^ 1 in M31, M33, and M74, to 9 km s ^ 1 in M51 and 18 km s ^ 1 in the Antennae.

sources.summary: The paper identifies LMC as an individually detected nearby ray source, removes it from the point-source SFG stack because it is extended, and notes its FIR luminosity is atypically low for the sample.

quotes.quote: While the majority of galaxies in our universe produce a ray flux far below the sensitivity of current instruments, a handful of galaxies have been individually detected. These include several of our closest neighbors (LMC, SMC and M31), as well as some of the most intense nearby star-forming galaxies (NGC 1068, NGC 3034 (M 82), NGC 4945, and Arp 220) Acero:2015hja, Peng:2016nsx .

Second, we have removed the LMC, SMC and M31 from this sample, as they are extended ray sources that may not be accurately modeled with the point-source analysis technique employed here. Additionally, the FIR luminosities for these systems are significantly smaller than the typical galaxies in our analysis. Detailed ray analyses of these galaxies exist in the literature 2010A&A...523L...2A, 2015PhRvD..91j2001B, 2015arXiv150103460P, 2016PhRvD..93f2004C , and we will compare our results to these systems in the discussion.

sources.summary: The paper uses LMC bar and disk stellar data to show a lower abundance-scaling breakpoint than the Milky Way and evidence for two stellar populations.

quotes.quote: The spectra of individual giant stars can also be measured for nearby galaxies, for example the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) 2008AA...480..379P, 2013AA...560A..44V and the Sculptor Dwarf Elliptical galaxy 2010AA...524A..58T, 2005AJ....129.1428G, 2009ApJ...705..328K . Figure fig9 shows the same data for [ Mg/Fe ] vs [ Fe/H ] as in Figure fig2 but including data for the Sculptor Dwarf and the LMC bar and disk.

The LMC stars also exhibit a lower break point than the Milky Way, due to lesser contributions to the metallicity from core-collapse supernovae, as suggested by . The LMC appears to have two populations as some stars have abundance ratios similar to the Milky Way stars, while others appear to be intermediate between the Milky Way and Sculptor.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as one of the interacting Magellanic Clouds and finds several Leading Arm stars chemically compatible with LMC material.

quotes.quote: The Magellanic Stream MS, math74, dong15 , including the Bridge kerr57, hind63 , and the Leading Arm (LA) is a well-known, 200^ long H 1 structure, evidencing the interaction between the Small and Large Magellanic Clouds (SMC and LMC, respectively) and the Milky Way (MW) nide10 . In recent years, comprehensive studies of these structures have been carried out to understand the complex interactions between the Clouds and the MW, and hence to provide constraints on the gravitational potentials of these galaxies.

Thus, our first conclusion as derived from Fig. fig:abun_res is that the environment in which our target stars formed is similar to that of the MW and the LMC, and unlike that of the more metal-poor SMC.

The metallicity as represented by Mg in our study indicates that the LA material is more metal poor than the Galactic disk (as represented by two stars at similar location with LA members), and compatible with the metallicity of the LMC (are represented by young cluster NGC 2004).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes four Large Magellanic Cloud H II regions with deep VLT spectra, reporting higher oxygen abundance than in the SMC, similar S/Cl/Ne behavior, and distinct nitrogen abundance tied to chemical evolution.

quotes.quote: We obtained echelle spectra of 8 H ii regions in the MCs, 4 in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) and 4 in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC).

We can see that the O abundance is higher for the LMC than the SMC by 0.38 dex. In Figure abund_fig3 we plot the total abundance for the other elements with respect to oxygen as a function of the ionization degree. The S and Cl abundances are similar in both clouds within the uncertainties; the same behavior is seen for the Ne abundances. For N abundances it seems to be a separation between the two MCs due to differences in the chemical evolution of each galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper derives LMC cluster-formation and age-metallicity patterns, finding a 5 Gyr formation period, a 3-10 Gyr age gap, and a metallicity drop around 3.8-3 Gyr ago.

quotes.quote: A distinct period of cluster formation is visible in the LMC around 5 Gyr, which culminated 1.3 Gyrs ago.

While the LMC sharply drops to almost zero from 1 Gyr to present times, the SMC shows a softer descent with smaller peaks around 250 Myr and 130 Myr. The well known ``age gap'' in the LMC between 3--10 Gyrs Balbinot_2010 is present, visible as a marked drop in the KDE_ (age/yr) curve at 9.5 dex.
For the LMC galaxy, Fig. fig:amr center plot, we see a marked drop in metallicity from 0.45 dex beginning around 3.8 Gyr, and ending 3 Gyrs ago at 0.6 dex.

sources.summary: The paper reports VMC coverage and public-release tiles for the LMC, uses its depth in distance work, and compares the de-projected SMC to an LMC-like barred galaxy with a spiral-arm region.

The fourth public data release of the VMC data comprises of 19 tiles ( 10 in the LMC, 5 in the SMC, 2 in the Bridge, and 2 in the Stream).

Considering that the depth of the Magellanic Clouds is about 0.2 mag for the LMC and 0.7 mag for the SMC, we are able to use these relations to quantify distance effects throughout the galaxies. Preliminary maps of the spatial distribution of classical Cepheids in the different combination of cartesian coordinates indicate that the de-projected SMC resembles the LMC galaxy with an elongated bar and a region above it that can be associated to a spiral arm.

sources.summary: The paper treats LMC as a massive Local Group actor whose mass improves orbit modeling, prefers twice the catalog mass, and assigns redshift and distance uncertainties for fitting.

quotes.quote: LMC is much less luminous than MW, but my experience suggests that when LMC is treated as massless it is more difficult to find a reasonable LMC orbit. The dynamical significance of the LMC mass is indicated by the model preference for LMC mass twice the catalog value.

sources.summary: The CTA dark-matter program describes the LMC as a nearby, face-on satellite with large dark-matter mass and proposes multi-pointing observations covering the full galaxy.

quotes.quote: ht The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) is a nearby satellite galaxy at high Galactic latitude and it has the shape of a disk seen nearly face-on. At a distance of only 50 kpc, and with a large dark matter mass of 10^ 10 M_ , the LMC has long been recognized as a potentially favorable target for indirect dark matter searches . For the LMC, observations will be taken with several pointings to cover the full galaxy. A total of 340 h of observations are proposed for both DM and astrophysical motivations.

sources.summary: The paper argues HVS3 was ejected from the Large Magellanic Cloud center, implying a Hills-mechanism kick and strong evidence for a central massive black hole.

quotes.quote: HVS3 is highly likely to be coming almost from the centre of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). During its closest approach, 21.1^ +6.1 _ 4.6 Myr ago, it had a relative velocity of 870^ +69 _ 66 kms ^ 1 with respect to the LMC. This large kick velocity is only consistent with the Hills mechanism, requiring a massive black hole at the centre of the LMC. This provides strong direct evidence that the LMC itself harbours a massive black hole of at least 4 10^3 -10^4 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a massive infalling dwarf galaxy whose stellar and dark matter mass should allow it to host ultra-faint satellite companions.

quotes.quote: Using semi-analytic models of galaxy formation Sales2013 determined the number of satellites expected around dwarf galaxies like the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) in LCDM theory (see also guo10,Guo2011 ). The LMC has a stellar mass of M_ 3 10^9 M_ vdM02 , implying a DM mass of log( M_ dark ) = 10.75--11.25 prior to capture by the MW moster13,NK13 . From Sales2013 , such LMC analogs should host one Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) analog (mass ratio 0.1 ) in addition to 5 -40 satellites that are 0.001 its mass, i.e., ultra-faint dwarf galaxies: M_ 0.1 - 1 10^4 M_ , log( M_ dark ) 8 .
The finding of 4 confirmed companions of the LMC, with perhaps 2 more, given the uncertainty in LMC mass, is consistent with the numbers expected for comparable mass systems in LCDM theory .

sources.summary: The paper models the Large Magellanic Cloud as a gravitational perturber that deflects hypervelocity-star trajectories and creates an overdensity along the line of sight toward the LMC.

quotes.quote: We consider how the gravity of the Galactic disk and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) modifies the radial motions of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Galactic Center.

For many lines-of-sight through the Galaxy, the LMC produces similar and sometimes larger deflections. Bound HVSs suffer larger deflections than unbound HVSs. Gravitational focusing by the LMC also generates a factor of two overdensity along the line-of-sight towards the LMC. With large enough samples, observations can detect the non-radial orbits and the overdensity of HVSs towards the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper uses Gaia DR2 proper motions to study young stars in the LMC outskirts, arguing that recent in-situ star formation occurred in the galaxy's periphery.

quotes.quote: We explore the Gaia DR2 proper motions of six young, main-sequence stars, members of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) reported by mb17 . These stars are located in the outskirts of the disk, between 7 and 13 from the LMC's center where there is very low H I content. Gaia DR2 proper motions confirm that four stars formed locally, in situ, while two are consistent with being expelled via dynamical interactions from inner, more gas-rich regions of the LMC. This finding establishes that recent star formation occurred in the periphery of the LMC, where thus far only old populations were known.

sources.summary: The paper includes the Large Magellanic Cloud as a dwarf irregular satellite and adopts literature estimates of its pre-infall halo mass.

quotes.quote: The satellite dwarf sample comprises the eight Milky Way `classical' dwarf spheroidals (dSphs): Draco, Fornax, UMi, Carina, Sextans, Sculptor, Leo I, and Leo II, the Sagittarius dSph, and the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) that is a dwarf irregular.

Finally, for the LMC and Sagittarius, we use \ values from the literature. The pre-infall halo mass of the LMC was recently estimated by 2016MNRAS.456L..54P using a timing argument. They found M_ 200, LMC = 0.25_ 0.08 ^ +0.09 10^ 12 M _ (a similar estimate has been determined recently by 2018arXiv181208192E using an entirely different analysis that models the deflection of the Tucana III stream due to the recent close passage of the LMC).

sources.summary: The paper uses the LMC as a nearby low-metallicity galaxy to study resolved star-forming ISM phases and velocity profiles along its molecular ridge.

quotes.quote: The aim of our study is to investigate the physical properties of the star-forming interstellar medium (ISM) in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) by separating the origin of the emission lines spatially and spectrally. The LMC provides a unique local template to bridge studies in the Galaxy and high redshift galaxies because of its low metallicity and proximity, enabling us to study the detailed physics of the ISM in spatially resolved individual star-forming regions. Following Okada \ et al. (2015, Paper I), we investigate different phases of the ISM traced by carbon-bearing species in four star-forming regions in the LMC, and model the physical properties using the KOSMA- \ PDR model. We mapped 3--13 arcmin ^2 areas in 30 Dor, N158, N160 and N159 along the molecular ridge of the LMC in \ 158 \ with GREAT on board SOFIA.

sources.summary: The LMC entry counts sixteen old globular clusters and treats the resulting globular-cluster system mass as a lower bound for the galaxy.

quotes.quote: The SMC and LMC Following 2010MNRAS.406.1967G , we include the single old GC (NGC 121) in the SMC, and the 16 old GCs in the LMC, to give the total mass of the GC system in each galaxy. We note that a number of Milky Way GCs have been suggested to once belong to the Magellanic Clouds, e.g. Rup 106 to the SMC 1995AJ....110.1664F . As such, our estimates of the GC system mass in the Clouds are likely to be lower bounds.

sources.summary: The paper frames the LMC as having a missing dust-mass problem, with ISM-tracing dust emission, long-term star formation, and literature dust masses near 10^6 solar masses.

quotes.quote: In parallel, closer to home, 2009MNRAS.396..918M pointed out a `missing dust-mass problem' in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) arising from the inability of existing AGB stars to produce the dust mass observed in the ISM.
Moreover, the dust emission in the LMC and SMC appears to trace the ISM gas rather than the stellar content of the galaxies 2013AJ....146...62M .

For the LMC, 2009AJ....138.1243H find that star formation has proceeded at an average rate of roughly 0.2 \ for the past 5 Gyr. 2012ApJ...761...42S find a dust mass of 1.1 10^ 6 , which appears consistent with 2009MNRAS.396..918M who quote a dust mass of 1.6 10^ 6 , based on the gas mass and an assumed dust-to-gas mass ratio.

sources.summary: The LMC is the only external galaxy whose nitrogen fractionation tightly constrains model parameters, favoring either 10^5 cm^-3 gas with standard cosmic rays or denser gas with elevated ionization.

quotes.quote: For the LMC, on the other hand, we are able to constrain the physical parameters much better, as there are only very few models that match the observations: a model with a gas density of 10 ^5 cm ^ 3 with a standard galactic cosmic ray ionization rate and an Av of > 10 mags, or models with a density of 10 ^6 cm ^ 3 , Av 10 mags and > 100 the standard value. In fact, the measured extinction in the LMC is significantly lower than the average found in the Milky Way (Dobashi et al. 2008), so the first case may be favoured. The radiation field is not constrained.

Our models succeed in reproducing the observed in external galaxies but due to the large ranges observed we are unable to fully constrain the physical parameters of each galaxy with the exception of the LMC whose nitrogen fractionation implies a gas density of 10 ^5 cm ^ 3 with galactic cosmic ray ionization rate and an Av of 100 mags, or a density of 10 ^6 cm ^ 3 , Av > 10 mags and > 100.

sources.summary: The paper applies machine-learning estimates to LMC Cepheids and RR Lyrae, builds fundamental-parameter catalogs, and derives LMC distance moduli of 18.688 mag from Cepheids and 18.52 mag from RR Lyrae.

quotes.quote: We apply this analysis to observations of hundreds Cepheids in the Large Magellanic Cloud and thousands of RR Lyrae in the Magellanic Clouds and Galactic bulge to produce catalogs of estimated masses, radii, luminosities, and other parameters of these stars. As an example application, we estimate Wesenheit indices and use those to derive distance moduli to the Magellanic Clouds of _ LMC , CEP = 18.688 0.093 , _ LMC , RRL = 18.52 0.14 , and _ SMC , RRL = 18.88 0.17  mag.
After sigma-clipping outliers at the 5 level, a weighted average of these distance moduli yields an LMC distance of 18.688 0.093  mag from Cepheids and 18.52 0.14  mag from RR Lyrae stars.

Catalogs Tables  tab:LMC-CEP , tab:LMC-RRL , tab:SMC-RRL , and  tab:BLG-RRL lists the estimated fundamental parameters for Cepheids in the LMC and RR Lyraes in the LMC, SMC, and Galactic Bulge, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a nearby dwarf-galaxy signal source whose black holes would produce a sky over-density at its bulk velocity, but whose peak strains are weaker and excluded from simulations.

quotes.quote: Closer to home, the Large (LMC) and Small Magellanic Clouds (SMC) are potential sources of signals. The contribution from either of these two dwarf galaxies would be an additional over-density of signals in a particular part of the sky and within a small range of Doppler shifts, since the black holes are traveling at the respective galaxy's bulk velocity (68 km/s for the LMC and 5 km/s for the SMC ). The LMC and SMC are an order of magnitude further than the Galactic Center , so the peak strains are an order of magnitude below the peak of the Milky Way distribution. Since the expected signal is small and the star formation histories of the LMC and SMC are very uncertain, we do not include them in our simulations.

sources.summary: The review identifies the Large Magellanic Cloud as the most massive Local Group dwarf with globular clusters, whose old metal-poor clusters may form a disk-like system.

quotes.quote: The most massive Local Group dwarf with GCs is the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) which has 16 known clusters. CMD and spectroscopic analysis indicates that they are old and metal-poor - a general property of GCs in dwarfs. Interestingly, the kinematics of these clusters suggest that they might be part of a disc system. This bears similarities to suggestions of "discy" GC systems seen in some spiral galaxies (Section LTGs ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Magellanic Cloud massive-star counts to estimate LMC B/R and WR/RSG ratios, finds BPASS underpredicts cloud metallicity, and infers larger initial binary or rotation fractions in higher-metallicity LMC than SMC.

quotes.quote: For both the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC, plotted in orange) and Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC, plotted in blue), we estimate R _ B/R using data from massey03 and R _ WR/RSG from massey03 , neugent11 , and neugent12 , and find R _ B/R = 13.521 ^ +0.937 _ 0.865 , R _ WR/RSG = 0.659 ^ +0.071 _ 0.064 in the LMC, and R _ B/R = 16.449 ^ +1.898 _ 1.658 , R _ WR/RSG = 0.134 ^ +0.046 _ 0.036 in the SMC. The Geneva models fail to reproduce the observations, while the BPASS models underpredict the metallicity of both clouds ( Z 0.001/0.002 for the SMC/LMC respectively). In both cases, the data indicate that the initial f_ bin or f_ rot is larger in the higher metallicity LMC than in the lower metallicity SMC, consistent with the results in dornwallenstein18 .

Measurements of the frequency of O star binaries (which in theory have not undergone mass transfer interactions in our model populations, and should serve as a decent proxy for the initial value of f_ bin ) reveal that f_ bin does indeed increase from the LMC ( f_ bin 0.5 , sana13 ) to the Galaxy ( f_ bin 0.7 , sana12 ).

sources.summary: The paper includes the Large Magellanic Cloud as a massive Milky Way satellite in Phoenix-stream orbit models, assigning it a Hernquist profile and testing fits with and without its influence.

quotes.quote: To determine its most likely orbit, we integrate numerical models over 3 billion years in a Milky Way potential including the effect of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), and attempt to reproduce the observed positions on the sky, radial velocities, and proper motions for all the high-likelihood \ members in our sample.
The LMC is modelled as an Hernquist profile hernquist_1990 with a mass of 1.5 10^ 11 M_ and a scale radius of 17.14 kpc, consistent with the recent measurement of the LMC mass orphan_lmc_modelling .

We also perform fits without the LMC and find that Phoenix can be accurately fit in either case. Thus, Phoenix is not as sensitive to the LMC as is the Orphan stream orphan_lmc_modelling,2019MNRAS.485.4726K .

sources.summary: The paper treats the Large Magellanic Cloud as a nearby, resolvable galaxy with a substantial Cepheid population and as the current anchor galaxy for the extragalactic distance scale.

quotes.quote: Of particular importance are the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC and SMC, respectively). These galaxies are relatively nearby, making them excellent targets for stellar population studies as their individual stars can be resolved. Notably, they each have a substantial Cepheid population, with the Optical Gravitational Lensing Experiment (OGLE) Udalski1999 cataloguing 5000 fundamental-mode Classical Cepheids across both galaxies. Due to the LMC being used as the current anchor galaxy for the extragalactic distance scale, its Cepheid population has been studied extensively.

sources.summary: The paper discusses the LMC as a massive Local Group member whose distance, mass estimates, and analogues significantly affect simulated MW-M31 orbital kinematics.

quotes.quote: In addition to M31 and the Milky Way, the Local Group contains two galaxies believed to be residing in haloes of at least 10^ 11 : M33, at a distance of 203 37 kpc from M31 vanderMarel-2012 , and the LMC at a distance of 50 kpc vanderMarel-2002 from the MW centre. With stellar masses of 3-5 10^9 , abundance matching places both in dark matter haloes of 10^ 11 .

For the LMC mass, timing constraints give a total mass of 2.5 10^ 11 Penarrubia-2015 ; analogues by stellar mass in the Eagle simulations indicate M _ 200 = 3.4^ +1.8 _ 1.2 10^ 11 Shao-2018 , while its effect on the Orphan stream in the Milky Way suggests a mass of 1.38^ +0.27 _ 0.24 10^ 11 Erkal-2019 .

This does not necessarily imply that the presence of LMC/M33 analogues is required to form a LG with the observed parameters (although it is necessary in our particular LSS constraints), but we can conclude that the presence of the LMC and M33 significantly affects the kinematics of the MW-M31 orbit.

sources.summary: The paper studies molecular-cloud physical conditions in the LMC Molecular Ridge, noting the Ridge holds much of the galaxy's CO-bright molecular gas and comparing fitted masses to LMC metallicity expectations.

quotes.quote: Through this model-fitting study, we determine the physical conditions of molecular clouds in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and compare those results to other common methods: adopting an factor, assuming LTE, or using line diagnostics. We use as a case study the quiescent southern end of the Molecular Ridge in the LMC, extending 1-2 kpcs south from 30 Doradus (Figure fig:LMC zoom in ). We assume a distance to the LMC of 50 kpc Schaefer08 . Cohen88 first noted the Ridge as a striking feature in low resolution maps of (1-0), and further observations of \ by the NANTEN survey NANTEN revealed that it contained 30 of all CO-bright molecular gas mass in the LMC Mizuno01 .

sources.summary: The review cites deep H observations of the LMC as evidence that low-surface-brightness outflow material may be missed in more distant galaxies.

quotes.quote: And this has been seen in a new study of the LMC using extremely deep H observations across the whole galaxy . The LMC has a lower sSFR than the star-bursting dwarfs presented in most other studies, and so may be assumed to have a lower mass-loading factor. However, in fig. fig:massloading (where the LMC is shown as a diamond) it is perfectly consistent with the higher FIRE-1 predictions. While this result is for one galaxy, which also has a range of assumptions made about the geometry and distribution of the wind, it makes a compelling case that we may not be capturing all the outflowing material in more distant galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper links an overdensity of Ce- and Nd-enhanced stars to LMC red supergiants, describes them as young metal-rich stars, and warns their derived abundances may be systematic.

quotes.quote: There is also a notable overdensity of Ce-enhanced stars (and Nd-enhanced stars) at a metallicity of -0.5, which appears to be predominantly due to red supergiants in the LMC. While LMC stars at higher metallicities may in general be enhanced in s-process elements e.g., Hasselquist2021 , we suspect the LMC red supergiants have suspect stellar parameters, leading to erroneous chemical abundances not only in Ce and Nd but other elements too, as discussed below in Section sec:lmc_rsg .

This feature is primarily from red supergiants (RSGs) in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). These stars are relatively metal-rich stars for the LMC, and because they are all young, recently formed stars, they only cover a narrow range of metallicity.

sources.summary: The paper measures a galaxy-wide OBe fraction of about 20 percent in the Large Magellanic Cloud and notes its stronger bright-end decline relative to lower-metallicity systems.

quotes.quote: We find OBe star fractions of 20 in the Large Magallanic Cloud (0.5 Z_ ), and 30 in the Small Magellanic Cloud (0.2 Z_ ) as well as in the so-far unexplored metallicity range 0.1 Z/Z_ < 0.2 occupied by the other three dwarf galaxies.

For the Small and Large Magellanic Cloud (SMC and LMC), we cross-correlate Using http://cdsxmatch.u-strasbg.fr/ the data from GAIA EDR3 Gaia21 , with classical UB photometry Zaritsky02, Zaritsky04 and infrared Spitzer-SAGE photometry Meixner06 , adopting a 1" radius. In the LMC, we use the same method to identify HeB, MS, and OBe stars, except that we place the threshold for HeB stars at U-B > -0.3 to account for redder intrinsic colors and higher extinction. This decrease is the strongest in the LMC, which could be related to stellar winds being relatively strong there (as the LMC is the highest-metallicity galaxy in our sample).

sources.summary: The review notes that the Magellanic Clouds uniquely have high-resolution stellar abundance samples among dwarf irregulars and provide CNO isotope constraints from LMC and SMC star-forming regions.

quotes.quote: With the notable exceptions of the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (hereinafter, LMC and SMC, respectively), for which statistically significant samples of (giant) stars with high-resolution spectra are available pomp08,lape12,nide20 , chemical abundances in dIrrs and BCDs are mainly measured from optical and NIR spectroscopy of bright H II regions see and references therein anni22 and UV nebular emission lines e.g., berg19 .

Turning to the minor CNO isotopes, abundance estimates of CNO isotopes in dwarf galaxies are available only for the Magellanic Clouds. From a spectral line survey of the prominent star-forming region N 113 in the LMC covering parts of the frequency range from 85 GHz to 357 GHz, ratios of ^ 12 C/ ^ 13 C  49 5 , ^ 16 O/ ^ 18 O  2000 250 , and ^ 18 O/ ^ 17 O  1.7 0.2 are estimated by . Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) observations in the ^ 12 CO( J = 2-1 ), ^ 13 CO( J = 2-1 ), C ^ 18 O( J = 2-1 ), ^ 12 CO( J = 3-2 ), and ^ 13 CO( J = 3-2 ) lines toward the active star-forming region N83C in the SMC are presented by mura17 , from which the ^ 12 C/ ^ 13 C and ^ 16 O/ ^ 18 O isotopic ratios can be derived.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a uniquely massive Milky Way satellite whose mass, recent pericentre, and effect on the Galactic halo make it a potential perturber of GSE debris.

quotes.quote: The two key exceptions to this are the uniquely massive Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) and the Sagittarius dwarf spheroidal galaxy (Sgr). The LMC has a total mass of around (1-2) 10^ 11 M _ , likely just past its first approach pericentre of about 50 kpc about the MW kallivayalil2006smc, erkal2019total . Given the LMC's close proximity as the MW's largest satellite, its effect on the Galactic halo has already been shown to be substantial erkal2021detection .
The unique behaviour of the LMC and Sgr means they may be able to have their orbits or total masses constrained by the discovered local phase space chevrons.

sources.summary: The paper compares the LMC chemical locus to GE/S, finding massive-satellite abundance behavior and noting that its mass exceeds both GE/S and the SMC.

quotes.quote: By and large, the locus occupied by GE/S stars in the Mg-Fe plane is somewhat similar to that where stars from the massive MW satellites LMC, SMC, and Sgr dSph are located see also Hasselquist2021 . The distributions however differ in important details. All three of the massive satellites show, at a given [ Fe/H ] , a positive change in the slope of the [ Mg/Fe ] [ Fe/H ] relation, whereby [ Mg/Fe ] starts increasing with increasing metallicity.

The contrast between the histories of star formation of GE/S and massive MW satellites, and in particular the LMC and SMC is interesting in light of the fact that the mass of GE/S, according to various studies, is of the order of a few to several times 10 ^8 M_ e.g., Lane2021,Deason2019,mackereth2019origin , which is comparable to that of the SMC ( 5 10^8 M_ ) and smaller than that of the LMC 1.5 10^9 M_ , mcconnachie2012observed .

sources.summary: The paper places R136 in the LMC and argues that ultraviolet extinction varies across the galaxy, motivating an LMC-wide study of grain and extinction relations.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, a comparison with average curves tailored to other regions of the LMC shows that large differences in ultraviolet extinction exist within this galaxy. Further investigation is required in order to decipher whether or not there is a relation between and ultraviolet extinction in the LMC.

It therefore remains unclear as to whether or not there exists a relation between the different grain populations in the LMC, or what the nature of this relation could be. It would be of value to carry out an LMC-wide study of the R_V dependence of UV extinction in order to investigate this further.

sources.summary: The review argues that the LMC is a massive Milky Way satellite whose high mass, orbit, satellites, and halo perturbations imply major dynamical effects.

quotes.quote: The LMC is undoubtedly the largest satellite of the Milky Way, but just how large it is only became apparent relatively recently (see Figure fig:lmc_mass for a compilation of estimates). The stellar mass of the LMC is 3 10^9 M_ vanderMarel2002 and gas mass 5 10^8 M_ Fox2014 , but its total mass ought to be significantly larger. There are several lines of evidence suggesting that the LMC mass is in the range (1-2) 10^ 11 M_ .

Perturbations of stellar streams and other kinematic tracers in the outer Milky Way halo are discussed at length in Section sec:dynamics , but they all are best explained if the LMC mass is in the range (1--2) 10^ 11 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper uses Magellanic Stream modelling to constrain the LMC mass below 10 ^ 11 M _ , links the North Tidal Arm to its disk, and favors a few-times-10 ^ 10 M _ LMC.

quotes.quote: In particular, this model requires a LMC mass significantly smaller than 10 ^ 11 M _ .

The North Tidal Arm (NTA) is the largest tidal features rooted from the disk LMC, which is confirmed to originate from the LMC on the basis of its metallicity, distance, and kinematics Cullinane2022a,Cullinane2022b,Cullinane2020 . Before observations, the model of Wang2019 predicted the existence of the NTA , which is formed by the Galactic tides exerted on the disk of LMC.
We conjecture that the LMC mass has to be small (a few times 10 ^ 10 M _ ) to form the Magellanic Stream, though further studies are needed to explore the exact mass range.

sources.summary: The paper gives the Large Magellanic Cloud a stellar mass of 1.6e9 solar masses and rising star-formation histories in both delayed and power-law models, similar to M33.

quotes.quote: The Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) with M_ * = 1.6 10^ 9 M_ and M33 with M_ * = 2.5 10^ 9 M_ have continuously rising SFHs in both the delayed- and power-law- SFH model. In the power-law- SFH model, the massive galaxies MW, M31, NGC 5128/Cen A, M81, IC 342, but also M32 have a decreasing, while the low-mass galaxies SMC, LMC, and M33 have an increasing SFH over cosmic time in agreement with e.g.

The LMC and M33 (Triangulum) galaxy have M_ * = 1.6 10^ 9 M_ and 2.5 10^ 9 M_ and SFR_ 0 = 0.3 M_ yr^ 1 and 0.5 M_ yr^ 1 , respectively. Therefore, both galaxies have a very similar increasing SFH in the delayed- and power-law- SFH model (Figure figure_SFHs ).

sources.summary: The paper describes the Large Magellanic Cloud as a massive companion, roughly one-sixth to one-fourth of the Milky Way mass, whose gravitational perturbations may torque the Milky Way halo and affect figure rotation.

quotes.quote: However, in recent years it has become increasingly clear that the influence of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), now believed to be between 1/6th and 1/4th the mass of the Milky way, is significantly perturbing the MW halo. These perturbations in the gravitational potential of the dark matter halo are thought to be both time-evolving and radius dependent garavito-camargo_quantifying_2021 , and have been shown to affect the structure and kinematics of several tidal streams erkal_total_2019, vasiliev_tango_2021,koposov_s_2023 . It is possible or even likely that the influence of the LMC is consequential for figure rotation in the MW dark halo. Depending on the initial orientation of the MW dark halo, the gravitational influence of the LMC could possibly torque the halo, which may in turn either enhance or disrupt figure rotation.

sources.summary: The paper says the LMC may have shifted the Milky Way disc relative to its halo and infers an LMC mass of 2-3 x 10^10 solar masses.

quotes.quote: Being at first passage, the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) may have affected the MW disc location with respect to its halo Conroy2021,Erkal2021 , but is unlikely to have affected its internal dynamics or morphology Laporte2018 .

A small dynamical mass for the MW may also impact mass estimations for the LMC, which is mostly constrained by its ratio to that of the MW; for example, when studying the induced sloshing of the Galactic halo Erkal2021,Conroy2021 . If the total MW mass is as small as 2.06 10^ 11 \ M_ , the LMC total mass would be from 2 to 3 10^ 10 M_ . Interestingly, the latter value is consistent with the modelling of the Magellanic Stream through ram pressure as shown by Hammer2015 and see also LMC mass predictions from Wang2022b Wang2019 , which would resolve the numerous difficulties in reproducing it with tidal tail models Besla2012,Lucchini2020,Lucchini2021 .

sources.summary: The paper uses the LMC as a distance anchor, citing its eclipsing-binary distance modulus and explaining that orbital velocities and light curves make the estimate insensitive to fifth-force effects.

quotes.quote: To summarize, for the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) we utilize a distance modulus of _ LMC =18.477 0.0263 derived from double eclipsing binaries Paczynski:1996dj,Pietrzy_ski_2019,Riess_2019 . The distance to N4258 is determined through mega-maser observations to _ N4258 =29.397 0.032 Reid_2019 . Data for the Milky Way (MW) Cepheids, including GAIA parallax measurements, are extracted from Table 1 in ref. . For Cepheids in the LMC, we obtain the relevant data from Table 2 in ref. , while for Cepheids in M31 and beyond, we use the data in Table 4 in ref. .

LMC. The distance to the LMC is estimated using observations of detached eclipsing binaries (DEBs). The distance is inferred from the orbital velocity and photometric light curve of the system, which provide information about the physical size of the individual stars .

sources.summary: The paper models the LMC's present-day location and orbit, identifying sky regions where reflex motion and leading LMC dark-matter subhalos alter stream-subhalo encounter probabilities.

quotes.quote: The system is rotated in such a way that the massive satellite is roughly in the same location as the real LMC at present day and also the orbits are approximately identical.

These regions are shaded manually by running a k-means clustering algorithm on stream location macqueen1967classification , dividing the stream positions in galactocentric coordinates into three distinct groups: one group for the background, another for reflex motion, and a third for leading LMC subhalos categories, while also removing any outliers. The red-shaded region represents the polar opposite region to the LMC, where the dominant effect on stream--subhalo interactions is the reflex motion response, both in position and velocity. The purple-shaded region indicates a higher subhalo density due to the presence of LMC dark matter subhalos in the leading debris and the LMC itself.

sources.summary: The paper describes the LMC as the best-studied Mira environment, central to Mira period-luminosity relations and a key extragalactic distance-ladder anchor.

quotes.quote: LMC The Mira variables in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) are the best studied and have informed our knowledge of Miras in environments where the data is sparser. The LMC has been a critical galaxy both in the history and development of Mira P-L relations and also in the extragalactic distance ladder, as an anchor galaxy (see Section sec:anchors ). With a small number of Miras from the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), created the first P-L relation for Miras using bolometric magnitude.

showed that P-L relations for O-rich Miras in the LMC exist in a wide range of wavelengths, from I band to [ 24 ] while C-rich Miras typically only exhibit a P-L relation at longer wavelengths (see Figure fig:PLRs ).

sources.summary: The paper uses DENIS red-giant-branch photometry to determine the LMC distance modulus, finding 18.55 with formal and systematic uncertainties and emphasizing the large, low-extinction sample.

quotes.quote: We combine the results of our algorithm with theoretical predictions to derive the distance modulus of the Magellanic Clouds. We obtain m-M = 18.55 0.04 (formal) 0.08 (systematic) for the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), and m-M = 18.99 0.03 (formal) 0.08 (systematic) for the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC). These are among the most accurate determinations of these quantities currently available, which is a direct consequence of the large size of our sample and the insensitivity of near infrared observations to dust extinction.
Among other things, this yields an important new determination of the distance modulus of the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper uses absorbed background AGN detected by XMM-Newton to probe interstellar gas in the LMC, comparing X-ray columns with H I data and inferring extra ionized/molecular gas or metal-abundance effects.

quotes.quote: The spectral indices and the probable identification of a radio counterpart for the brightest source suggest AGN shining through the interstellar gas of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). The column densities derived from the X-ray spectra in combination with H i measurements will allow to draw conclusions on H i to H _2 ratios in the LMC and compare these with values found for the galactic plane.

Kim et al. ( ) have produced a H i column density map of the LMC with a resolution of 1 \ which shows variations from 0 to 5 21 with maximum values around the 30 Doradus region. The higher absorption seen in the XMM sources suggests either large amounts of H ii and/or H _2 gas or higher metal abundances along their lines of sight through the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper measures Stromgren photometric reddening in two LMC fields, finding foreground E(B-V)=0.085, intrinsic Cloud reddening up to 0.21, and random small-scale variation across the fields.

quotes.quote: The individual reddenings for B stars in two fields in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) and two fields in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) are determined by means of Str o mgren uvby CCD photometry. In both LMC fields we find a foreground reddening of E(B-V) = 0.085 0.02 , and for the SMC fields we find E(B-V) = 0.070 0.02 . In addition to the foreground reddening we find contributions from reddening intrinsically in the Clouds up to E(B-V) 0.21 . The intrinsic contribution is not correlated with position within our 6 4 5 CCD fields but varies in an essentially random way.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as an orbiting satellite whose inclined orbit excites a halo wake that can differentially torque the Milky Way disk and drive warp-like asymmetries.

quotes.quote: For example, Weinberg (1998b) suggests the possibility of a warp created by a distortion from the orbiting LMC. The LMC first excites a wake in the halo. The halo distortion will be similar in shape to those depicted in Figures fig:halodamp and fig:densfluct . But because the LMC orbital plane is highly inclined this wake can differentially distort the disk plane and the disk response is a warp (more on this in sec:LMC ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Mira period-luminosity calibration to derive an LMC distance modulus of 18.60, consistent with Cepheid and globular-cluster based comparisons.

quotes.quote: The mean of these two estimates together with data on LMC Miras yields an LMC distance modulus of 18.60 0.10 in evident agreement with a metallicity corrected Cepheid modulus (18.59 0.10).

The line is the LMC Mira PL relation (equation 1) with = 0.89 , where we have adopted a Cepheid distance modulus for the LMC of 18.59. This Cepheid modulus depends on LMC V,I photometry, a galactic zero-point calibration using parallaxes, proper motions and radial velocities, pulsation parallaxes and Cepheids in galactic clusters, together with a metallicity correction (Feast 2001a) The Cepheid metallicity correction for V,I photometry remains somewhat uncertain, though it seems unlikely to be significantly larger for the LMC than that used by Feast (2001a).
It is evident in Fig. \ 1 that the agreement in the PL diagram of LMC field Miras with those in Galactic Globular Clusters and also the Mira in NGC 121 (SMC) is good.

sources.summary: The paper models microlensing toward the LMC using its source-star luminosity function and inner surface brightness, and discusses distinguishing MACHOs from stars in the LMC.

quotes.quote: For the luminosity function of the source stars in the LMC, _ L (V) , we follow equations (15) and (16) in with the IMF index _ s = 2.35 , which is consistent with the luminosity function of the LMC observed by using the HST ( hol97 ).

We adopt the background flux f_ b = 1200 e ^- s ^ 1 corresponding to V=20.6 mag, which is estimated as follows: the mean V-band surface brightness of the inner 10 deg ^2 of the LMC is V=22.01 mag arcsec ^ 2 , and the sky value is V= 21.6 mag arcsec ^ 2 ( vau57 ).

One could also distinguish whether the lenses are MACHOs or stars in the LMC itself through finite source size effects measurements in EAGLE events ( sum00 ). Therefore one could identify lens objects as halo MACHOs, dark stars in the Galactic thick disc, or stars in the LMC through these observations.

sources.summary: The review uses LMC Cepheids to calibrate Cepheid period-luminosity relations, derive new optical and infrared PL relations, and adopt an LMC distance modulus of 18.55 +/- 0.06.

quotes.quote: It is the purpose of this review to contribute such progress, and our approach is to combine Galactic and LMC Cepheids in the best possible way to derive both an improved absolute calibration of the PL relation in a number of optical and near-infrared photometric bands and, in a parallel step, derive an improved distance to the Large Magellanic Cloud from its Cepheid variables. One of the reasons why a PL calibration from Galactic Cepheid variables is of advantage as compared to a calibration based on LMC Cepheids alone is the fact that in most large spiral galaxies, and in particular in those targeted by the HST Key Project, the mean metallicities are quite close to solar, implying that metallicity-related systematic effects are minimized when comparing these extragalactic PL relations to the Galactic one, rather than to the one defined by the more metal-poor population of LMC Cepheids.

In order to make things fully comparable, we have derived new LMC Cepheid PL relations in the optical ( VIW ) from the published OGLE2 database, and in the infrared ( JHK ) from the sample kindly provided by M. Groenewegen, adopting the same reddening law as for our Galactic calibrators. From this procedure we find, as our best adopted value, a LMC distance modulus of 18.55 0.06 .

sources.summary: The paper uses the C/M AGB-star ratio to map metallicity in the Large Magellanic Cloud, finding a radial decrease and clumpy variations tied to about 0.75 dex metallicity spread.

quotes.quote: Regions of different metallicity have been identified in the Magellanic Clouds by using the ratio between Asymptotic Giant Branch stars of spectral type C and M. In the Large Magellanic Cloud the ratio appears to decrease radially while in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) there is no clear trend, reflecting either the large extension of the SMC along the line of sight or a more complex star formation history. The distribution of the C/M ratio is clumpy and corresponds to a spread in [ Fe/H ] of 0.75 dex in both Clouds. There is an indication of increasing C/M ratio, thus decreasing metallicity, towards the Bridge region connecting the two Clouds.

sources.summary: The chapter contrasts the LMC HMXB population with the SMC, describing it as Milky-Way-like in spectral distribution and formed through moderately massive binaries.

quotes.quote: In complete contrast, the HMXB population of the LMC seems very representative of the sample found in the Milky Way. A comprehensive review by Negueruela Coe (2002) of all identified optical counterparts in the LMC found that the overall spectral distribution of the population looks very similar to that of our Galaxy (Figure lmc ). They found that the spectral distribution for BeX binaries in both the Milky Way and the LMC is sharply peaked at spectral type B0 (roughly corresponding to M_ * 16 M_ ) and not extending beyond B2 ( M_ * 10 M_ ), which strongly supports the existence of supernova kicks. So the BeX binaries in the LMC must have preferentially formed from moderately massive binaries undergoing semi-conservative evolution in the same manner as most galactic systems.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes LMC microlensing candidates, finding fewer confirmed events than MACHO and arguing that known stellar populations in the outer Galaxy/LMC may explain them.

quotes.quote: We present a new analysis of the microlensing candidates towards the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC). The neural networks confirm the microlensing nature of only 7 of the possible 17 events identified by the MACHO experiment. This suggests that earlier estimates of the microlensing optical depth towards the LMC may have been overestimated. A smaller number of events is consistent with the assumption that all the microlensing events are caused by the known stellar populations in the outer Galaxy/LMC.

sources.summary: The paper links the Magellanic Clouds' longitudes and modeled orbits, especially the LMC's proximity and eccentric orbit, to possible perturbations of the outer Milky Way arm.

quotes.quote: It is interesting that these longitudes agree well with the Galactic longitudes of the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC, SMC) and the Leading Arm of the Magellanic Stream. In models of the orbits of the Magellanic Clouds e.g. gardiner96 , the Clouds should cross the Galactic plane around l=280 . The relative proximity of the LMC, in particular, should have a significant dynamical effect on the outer Milky Way disk, and may create a velocity perturbation in the outer spiral arm. For example, models of the effects of the Clouds on the Milky Way, such as that by weinberg95 , show that the eccentric orbit of the LMC can create radial velocity shifts on the order of 5 - 10 \ beyond the solar circle and in the direction of the Clouds.

sources.summary: The paper presents ATCA 4.8 and 8.6 GHz images of the entire LMC, resolving SNRs, H II regions, and smooth radio emission for multiwavelength studies.

quotes.quote: Detailed 4.8- and 8.6-GHz radio images of the entire Large Magellanic Cloud with half-power beamwidths of 33 at 4.8 GHz and 20 at 8.6 GHz have been obtained using the Australia Telescope Compact Array. A total of 7085 mosaic positions were used to cover an area of 6 ^ on a side. Full polarimetric observations were made. These images have sufficient spatial resolution ( 8 and 5 pc, respectively) and sensitivity (3 of 1 mJy beam ^ 1 ) to identify most of the individual SNRs and H II regions and also, in combination with available data from the Parkes 64-m telescope, the structure of the smooth emission in that galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper models LMC TRGB distances and finds that younger RGB populations bias metallicity and distance estimates; corrections to Magellanic Cloud relative distances depend on the TRGB technique and bolometric correction scale.

quotes.quote: Here we have investigated the reliability of the widely used I band Tip of the Red Giant Branch (TRGB) relative distances for a sample of Local Group galaxies with complex Star Formation Histories (SFRs) and Age Metallicity Relationships (AMRs) namely the LMC, SMC and LGS3. The use of the K band is also discussed. By employing theoretical stellar population synthesis techniques, we find that using actual determinations of SFR and AMR of the LMC and SMC, their RGB is populated by stars much younger (by 9 Gyr) than the Galactic globular cluster counterparts, on which the I band (and K band) TRGB absolute magnitude is calibrated. This age difference induces a bias in both the photometric metallicity estimates based on the comparison of RGB colours with globular cluster ones, and the TRGB distances. The extent of the distance bias -- which is not influenced by the actual value of the TRGB absolute magnitude zero point -- is strongly dependent on the specific TRGB technique applied, and on the assumed I band bolometric correction ( BC_I ) scale adopted; the correction to apply to the SMC--LMC distance modulus ranges from 0 mag up to +0.10 mag.

sources.summary: The paper uses LMC red giant variables to trace period-luminosity sequences, the RGB tip, and the LMC bar structure, finding the east side closer and a 2.4 kpc distance variation.

quotes.quote: Fig. \ pls shows the P--L relations from multiperiodic light curve fits of variable red giants in the LMC and SMC (Kiss Bedding 2003, 2004). The same parallel P--L sequences can be seen in both galaxies, which means that overall pulsation properties do not depend strongly on metallicity. The sharp discontinuity at K_S 12.05 (LMC) and K_S 12.70 (SMC) is exactly at the tip of the Red Giant Branch (TRGB).

In Fig. \ lmc3d we show the structure traced by 4,276 RGB pulsators in the LMC bar, which is in excellent agreement with the view based on other distance indicators (Cepheids, red clump stars, etc.): the east side of the LMC is closer than the west, while the inclination angle of the LMC bar is 30 ^ and the distance variation across the face of the LMC is measured at 2.4 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper describes LMC distance measurements from detached eclipsing binaries, noting a modern HV2274 determination, evidence for line-of-sight depth, agreement with other methods, and a current distance modulus estimate.

quotes.quote: The first modern and accurate determination of the LMC distance modulus by a detached eSB2, the Harvard Variable HV2274, is due to gui98 , previous attempts having essentially an historical interest. Since then, a few other systems have been studied, providing clues of a non-negligible depth of the Cloud along the line of sight gui04 . A parallel analysis of binaries in the Small Magellanic Cloud has been undertaken by hhh03 . In both cases the binary-based results are in excellent agreement with the determinations by other means and have an accuracy comparable to that of the best indicators. The latest estimate for the LMC, according to gui05 is (m-M)_0=18.42 0.07 , in good agreement with the value to which the various other results seem to converge (the weighted average from different methods, according to alv04 is 18.50 0.02 ).

sources.summary: The paper uses young and intermediate-age LMC clusters to probe the galaxy's structure, recent evolution, and cluster-formation history, noting its large cluster population and age-distance trends.

quotes.quote: Star clusters in the Magellanic Clouds can help us to have a better insight about the star formation history of the galaxies as a whole m98,psc01,psg02 . In particular, the large number of young and intermediate-age star clusters in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) composes an ensemble which allows to probe the galaxy structure and evolution during the last few Gyrs. Indeed, the total estimated number of clusters in the LMC is 4200 h88 , which compares to the catalogued 4089 clusters, associations with characteristics of somewhat looser clusters and newly-formed ones bsdo99 . Episodes of enhancement or reduction in cluster formation can be traced back in the galaxy's history if statistically meaningful samples of clusters with well-determined properties are considered.

sources.summary: The paper treats massive star clusters in the LMC as counterparts of old metal-rich globular clusters and compares LMC cluster and disk-star metallicities near [Fe/H] about -0.5.

quotes.quote: I argue that MSCs (compact populous and super--star clusters with M 10^4M_ ) in the LMC and other irregulars are counterparts of the old MRGCs and that another reason (quantitative and qualitative changes of the dust?) leads to (favors) their formation.

According to , a mean metallicity of the populous star clusters formed in the LMC 1--3 Gyr ago is close to [ Fe/H ] = -0.5, irrespective of their age and location in the galaxy. However, metallicity of the field stars near these clusters exhibits obvious dependence on age (see Fig.  agemet , where squares and asterisks are data from and , respectively). Moreover, the MDF for the disk stars of the LMC is virtually identical with that for the old red giant stars in the halo of NGC 5128 and M31 ( ), reaching its maximum somewhere near [ Fe/H ] 0.5 .

The difference between the age-metallicity relations for MSCs and stars in the LMC implies (provided it is the same in spheroids) different concentrations of MRGCs and stars to the centers of spheroids even under negligible GC disruption in the central parts and no merger of gas-rich spirals.

sources.summary: The paper models how tidal interactions trigger recent galaxy-wide star formation in the LMC, producing asymmetric high-density spiral arms and enhanced activity near the stellar bar and 30 Doradus.

quotes.quote: We discuss how tidal interaction between the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC), and the Galaxy triggers galaxy-wide star formation in the Clouds for the last 0.2 Gyr based on our chemodynamical simulations on the Clouds. Our simulations demonstrate that the tidal interaction induces the formation of asymmetric spiral arms with high gas densities and consequently triggers star formation within the arms in the LMC. Star formation rate in the present LMC is significantly enhanced just above the eastern edge of the LMC's stellar bar owing to the tidal interaction.

The location of the enhanced star formation is very similar to the observed location of 30 Doradus, which suggests that the formation of 30 Doradus is closely associated with the last Magellanic collision about 0.2 Gyr ago.

sources.summary: The paper uses LMC planetary nebula abundances to compare low-metallicity stellar yields, noting expanded ultraviolet carbon measurements and milder oxygen depletion than in SMC planetary nebulae.

quotes.quote: For the LMC, among about 350 PN known [ 14 ] only 20 IUE spectra are available, with only 10 sound carbon abundance determinations [ 13 ] . We increased this sample by observing 24 LMC PN with STIS [ 15 ] .

The STIS UV spectra of LMC PN show that the volume where the carbon emission originates is the same that the volume where the hydrogen recombination lines and the oxygen forbidden lines originate as well [ 15 ] , and the morphology of the nebulae is the same through the UV and the optical lines.
It is remarkable how the oxygen gets depleted in nitrogen rich type I SMC PN, while the effect is much milder in the LMC PN.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC FIR colours as part of the main dust-colour correlation, noting their upward shift and linking that position to a stronger interstellar radiation field.

quotes.quote: It is worth noting that the main correlation can also explain the FIR colours in the Magellanic Clouds. Since the LMC has a face-on geometry, the inhomogeneity in the ISRF in a line of sight may be small. The fact that the FIR colour-colour relation of the LMC shifts upward relative to that of the Galaxy suggests that the location in the main correlation reflects the strength of ISRF, since the LMC is generally believed to have a higher ISRF than the Galaxy e.g. fukui01,tumlinson02 . It is surprising that the FIR colours of the LMC and the SMC are located on the main correlation defined by the Galactic dust grains, and it is interesting to give a unified understanding of the FIR colours for those three galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper uses the Large Magellanic Cloud as the satellite-scale benchmark for simulations and concludes that the LMC likely experienced significant orbital energy and angular-momentum loss by dynamical friction.

quotes.quote: We opt for a simpler approach and fix the mean density of each satellite: _s = 2.73 kpc M_s 10^ 10 ^ 1/3 , The scaling is set so that a satellite with mass similar to the Large Magellanic Cloud (hereafter LMC) has a softening length comparable to the LMC's effective radius (de Vaucouleurs Freeman 1972).

The mass and size of the satellite in model 3 correspond closely to that of the LMC. It is clear from a comparison of Models 1,2, and 3 that dynamical friction by the galactic halo is negligible for satellites with masses 10^ 9 . Thus, globular clusters and the dwarf spheroidals in the galactic halo are not expected to have undergone significant changes in their orbital properties induced by dynamical friction by the halo; the only two structures in the galactic halo that have experienced significant amounts of dynamical friction by the halo are the LMC and the SMC, with masses of 2 10^ 10 and 2 10^9 respectively (Schommer 1992).

When scaling the simulations to represent the orbiting of satellites in the galactic halo, we find that the LMC and the SMC are the only objects in the outer Milky Way halo that have experienced significant amounts of energy and angular momentum loss by dynamical friction.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as the microlensing target, arguing that ordinary-star lensing would require extra stellar structure along its line of sight and would bias source magnitudes.

quotes.quote: There are lines of evidence suggesting that some of the observed microlensing events in the direction of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) are caused by ordinary star lenses as opposed to dark Machos in the Galactic halo. Efficient lensing by ordinary stars generally requires the presence of one or more additional concentrations of stars along the line of sight to the LMC disk. If such a population behind the LMC disk exists, then the source stars (for lensing by LMC disk objects) will be drawn preferentially from the background population and will show systematic differences from LMC field stars. One such difference is that the (lensed) source stars will be farther away than the average LMC field stars, and this should be reflected in their apparent baseline magnitudes.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as the most massive Milky Way satellite, estimates its accreted mass below 4 x 10^10, and notes its motion suggests first passage.

quotes.quote: The most massive Milky Way satellite, the LMC, was likely accreted with a mass no larger than m 4 10^ 10 vandermarel02,robertson05 . According to Figure NgtM , we expect that the Milky Way has accreted at least 3 objects that are larger than the LMC over its history.
Interestingly, a recent re-analysis of the LMC's motion suggests that it is indeed on its first passage about the Milky Way besla07 , as we would expect for surviving, massive satellites.

sources.summary: The paper discusses the LMC through Constellation III's setting: LMC 4 is a kiloparsec-scale HI-disk hole, the galaxy lacks comparable structures, and it contains a giant molecular ridge.

quotes.quote: In addition, Constellation III is embedded inside the supergiant shell LMC 4, a circular hole in the LMC's HI disk that spans more than a kiloparsec kim99 , and whose rim is dotted with HII regions mea80 .
After all, the LMC is home to thousands of star clusters which must have hosted similarly strong massive-star winds, yet it contains no other structures like Constellation III.

However, the LMC currently contains an example of just such a large, coherent prestellar cloud complex: there is a ridge of molecular gas extending more than 1.5 kpc southward from 30 Doradus, with a typical width of 100 pc miz01 .

sources.summary: The survey maps the LMC for methanol and excited-state OH masers, finds a new methanol maser in N160a, and reports underabundant LMC maser populations.

quotes.quote: The observations yielded the discovery of a fourth 6668-MHz methanol maser in the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), found towards the star-forming region N160a, and a second 6035-MHz excited-state hydroxyl maser, found towards N157a.
The LMC maser populations are demonstrated to be smaller than their Milky Way counterparts.
The current lower limits on the LMC maser populations are demonstrated to be up to a factor of 45 smaller than the Milky Way, with the methanol showing the largest discrepancy.

sources.summary: The paper finds an LMC metallicity gradient out to 8 kpc and interprets its populations as evidence for two major star-forming episodes and an LMC origin for the Stream.

quotes.quote: The metallicity of the LMC decreases linearly as 0.047 0.003 dex kpc ^ 1 out to 8 kpc from the centre.

The metallicity of the LMC, as traced by different populations, bears the signature of two major star forming episodes: the first one constituting a thick disc/halo population and the second one a thin disc and bar due to a close encounter with the MW and SMC.
The [ Fe/H ] of the recent episode supports an LMC origin for the Stream.

sources.summary: The paper describes the LMC's rich star cluster system and 3-10 Gyr age gap, then images candidate intermediate-age clusters to test whether they fill that gap.

quotes.quote: The LMC has a rich star cluster system spanning a wide range of ages and masses. One striking feature of the LMC cluster system is the existence of an age gap between 3-10 Gyrs. Four LMC clusters whose integrated colours are consistent with those of intermediate age simple stellar populations have been imaged with the Optical Imager (SOI) at the Southern Telescope for Astrophysical Research (SOAR).

sources.summary: The paper uses the LMC as a barred Local Group dwarf on a likely first-passage, extended orbit whose stellar component may remain in an early disk-to-spheroid transformation stage.

quotes.quote: One irrefutable example of a LG dwarf that contains a bar is the LMC. Orbital evolution models using three-dimensional velocities constrained by recent proper motion measurements Kallivayalil_etal06 suggest that the LMC may currently be on the first passage having just crossed the pericenter of its orbit around the MW Besla_etal07 . Confirming that the LMC is on a such extended orbit with a pericentric distance of r_ peri 50 kpc would be in line with our findings, as we expect its stellar component to be less evolved and thus in some early, transitory stage of the transformation between a disk and a spheroid.

sources.summary: The paper uses the LMC as one of two Magellanic Cloud galaxies for comparing massive-star infrared properties, finding fewer Oe/Be stars than in the SMC.

quotes.quote: We present a study of the infrared properties of 4922 spectroscopically confirmed massive stars in the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds, focusing on the active OB star population.
The LMC spectral type catalog contains 1750 massive stars.
These preliminary statistics (available for the first time for Oe stars) indicate that both Oe and Be stars are twice as common in the SMC than in the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper says the LMC is much more massive than typical modeled dwarfs, so it can retain warm gas against ram-pressure stripping, with tidal interactions potentially removing material.

quotes.quote: Under our model, the LMC and SMC being much more massive than the typical dwarf--- M_ LMC 2 10^ 10 M _ Schommer1992 , M_ SMC 3 10^9 M _ Harris2006 --will be able to retain significant quantities of warm gas against ram-pressure stripping. This protection from stripping would allow a significant quantity of gas to survive until the present day, with only tidal interactions---potentially those between the LMC and SMC---removing material from either dwarf galaxy. Although tidal stripping is included in our model, it is too simplistic to model the multi-body system present in the Magellanic clouds, with hydrodynamical simulations required to simulate the LMC-SMC-Galaxy system Lin1995,JBH2007,Nidever2008,Besla2010 .

sources.summary: The paper models the LMC as a recently interacting Magellanic companion whose orbit with the SMC shaped the Stream and whose chemical evolution was affected by accreted SMC material.

quotes.quote: We present an N-body model that reproduces the morphology and kinematics of the Magellanic Stream (MS), a vast neutral hydrogen (HI) structure that trails behind the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC and SMC, respectively) in their orbit about the Milky Way. After investigating 8 10^6 possible orbits consistent with the latest proper motions, we adopt an orbital history in which the LMC and SMC have only recently become a strongly interacting binary pair.
Insights into the chemical evolution of the LMC are also provided, as a substantial fraction of the material stripped away from the SMC is engulfed by the LMC.

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as a satellite with uncertain center and young globular clusters, estimating a possible intermediate-mass black hole but allowing none.

quotes.quote: LMC The Large Magellanic Cloud, being a satellite galaxy, is clearly different from the other systems in the sample used to derive the relation. The centre is not well defined and its globular clusters are not old. However, since the kinematics of the GC system is available ( Freeman1993 ), we simply mention that assuming the relation can be applied it predicts = 4.1 \ 5.4 \ 10^4 M_ which hints towards an intermediate mass black hole but is also consistent with the LMC having no central black hole.

sources.summary: The paper says the LMC has velocity near the Milky Way escape speed, has been modeled with the SMC as a first-passage satellite, and may be unbound.

quotes.quote: In addition, recent proper motion measurements of the Small and Large Magellanic Clouds (SMC/LMC) ( kallivayalil06a ; kallivayalil06b ) suggest that the total velocities of these clouds approach the escape speed of the Milky Way. showed that under the assumption of a Milky Way with virial mass M_ vir 10^ 12 M_ , the LMC and SMC are on their first passage about the Milky Way. The current results seem to increase the likelihood that the SMC/LMC are unbound.

sources.summary: The paper models the LMC TP-AGB stellar population with CB* simulations, using LMC metallicity and star formation history to compare luminosity and color distributions against observations.

quotes.quote: We show that Montecarlo simulations of the TP-AGB stellar population in the LMC and SMC galaxies using the CB ^ * models produce LF and color distributions that are in closer agreement with observations than those obtained with the BC03 and CB07 models.

We model the distribution of TP-AGB stars in the CMD in various optical and NIR bands for a stellar population of Z = 0.008 , close to the LMC metallicity, by means of Montecarlo simulations (cf. , 2010). At each time step the mass formed in stars is derived from the LMC star formation history ( ).

sources.summary: The paper treats the LMC as the SMC's interacting companion, linking SMC star-formation bursts to tidal capture by the LMC and noting a metal-rich LMC population associated with that episode.

quotes.quote: The SMC has experienced a complex and violent star formation history, because it is gravitationally bound with the LMC and the Milky Way, forming a triple system.

This scenario seems to be in agreement with the star formation history of the SMC derived by , where approximately 50 of the SMC stars formed in early epochs (at ages > 8 Gyr ago); (2) a secondary peak at [ Fe/H ] -0.6 dex, probably linked to the star formation burst occurring in the last few Gyr and due to the tidal capture of the SMC by the LMC ( ). This burst is also responsible of the formation of some intermediate-age SMC globular clusters (like NGC 419) and of the metal-rich ( [ Fe/H ] = --0.5 dex) population observed in the metallicity distribution of the LMC ( ); (3) a metal-poor tail, including three giants in the field of NGC 121, with [ Fe/H ] -2.5 dex.

sources.summary: The paper compares LMC dust production with AGB yield models, finding efficient carbon-star mass loss is needed and AGB stars may supply much of its dust mass.

quotes.quote: We compare theoretical dust yields for stars with mass 1 M_ m_ star 8 M_ , and metallicities 0.001 Z 0.008 with observed dust production rates (DPR) by carbon-rich and oxygen-rich Asymptotic Giant Branch (C-AGB and O-AGB) stars in the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds (LMC, SMC). The measured DPR of C-AGB in the LMC are reproduced only if the mass loss from AGB stars is very efficient during the carbon-star stage. The same yields over-predict the observed DPR in the SMC, suggesting a stronger metallicity dependence of the mass-loss rates during the carbon-star stage.
